history: Laguna Seca opened in 1957 and hosted Can-Am, CART, and major American sports car racing events across its history. The Corkscrew became famous worldwide through its uniqueness and the drama it has generated — a corner where the run-off is essentially non-existent and an error means leaving the circuit entirely. The circuit is located on public county land and has faced closure threats at various points due to noise complaints from the nearby Monterey area — the ongoing commercial and community tension is a defining feature of the circuit's modern operation.
notable_moments: The 1996 CART race where Alex Zanardi famously stuck his nose into The Corkscrew at the last moment to pass Bryan Herta for the lead — one of the greatest overtaking moves in American motorsport history. Valentino Rossi's wins at Laguna Seca during the MotoGP era when the circuit hosted the US Grand Prix. Multiple IMSA and IndyCar battles through The Corkscrew that have become defining images of American road racing.

name: Las Vegas Motor Speedway
bio: Las Vegas Motor Speedway's 1.5-mile D-oval sits north of the Strip in the Nevada desert. NASCAR has raced here since 1998; the consistent concrete surface and wide groove produce clean, high-speed oval racing.
overview: Las Vegas Motor Speedway is a 1.5-mile D-shaped oval on the northern outskirts of Las Vegas, Nevada. It serves a different purpose from the Las Vegas Strip Circuit used by F1 — LVMS is the traditional home of NASCAR and IndyCar in Nevada, a permanent 1.5-mile oval with the Las Vegas entertainment district's neon glow visible on the horizon from the grandstands. The spring NASCAR Cup race here has historically been one of the more popular stops on the schedule.
history: Las Vegas Motor Speedway opened in 1996 and joined the NASCAR Cup schedule in 1998. The facility is part of a broader motorsport complex that includes a short track, a drag strip, and a road course, making it one of the most comprehensive motorsport facilities in the United States. NASCAR's spring Las Vegas race became a fixture on the Cup schedule and later a playoff round. IndyCar held oval races at LVMS for several years.
notable_moments: Jeff Gordon's first Las Vegas Motor Speedway Cup victory. Kyle Busch's multiple wins at his home track — Las Vegas born and raised. The Camping World 400 weekends that bring the NASCAR circus to the entertainment capital of America.

name: Las Vegas Strip Circuit
bio: The Las Vegas Strip Circuit debuted in 2023, running through the neon-lit heart of the Las Vegas Boulevard. At night, with casinos as a backdrop, it is the most visually spectacular circuit in modern Formula 1.
overview: The Las Vegas Grand Prix Circuit is a 6.201 km street circuit running down and around the Las Vegas Strip — the most famous stretch of hotel and casino architecture in the world. Debuting in November 2023, it is the longest street circuit in current F1 use. The circuit passes Caesar's Palace, the Bellagio fountains, MSG Sphere, and numerous landmark resorts, with the main straight running the length of the Strip where F1 cars reach over 340 km/h. The night race format — racing begins at 10pm local time to benefit European morning television — positions Vegas as an entertainment event as much as a sporting one.
history: Las Vegas returned to the F1 calendar for the first time since the 1982 Caesars Palace Grand Prix — itself a brief and largely unsuccessful experiment. The 2023 revival was a Formula One Management promotion rather than a third-party promoted event, reflecting F1's direct commercial investment in the American market. The infrastructure investment was extraordinary: building a 55,000-seat main grandstand, paving or re-paving significant sections of the Strip, and installing the temporary circuit took months of preparation.
notable_moments: The inaugural 2023 Las Vegas Grand Prix, preceded by a drain cover incident in the first practice session that damaged multiple cars and caused session cancellation. Max Verstappen's 2023 victory. The extraordinary visual of F1 cars racing past lit casino façades at midnight. The circuit's status as one of F1's most commercially significant events in the American market expansion.

name: EuroSpeedway Lausitz
bio: EuroSpeedway Lausitz in eastern Germany is a hybrid facility — a full oval, a road course, and a combined layout sharing the same infrastructure. Built in 2000, it has struggled to find a permanent place in the European calendar.
overview: EuroSpeedway Lausitz — the Lausitzring — is a motorsport complex in Brandenburg, eastern Germany, featuring both an oval (2.222 km) and a road course (4.971 km). Built on former lignite mining land in the Lusatian coal district, it opened in 2000 with ambitions to be one of Europe's premier motorsport facilities. Today it hosts DTM rounds on its road course as a regular fixture of the German touring car calendar.
history: EuroSpeedway Lausitz was built as part of a post-reunification investment in the formerly industrial eastern German Lausitz region. The facility opened in 2000 with both oval and road course capabilities, designed to attract American oval racing as well as European circuit events. CART visited in 2001, a weekend marred by Alex Zanardi's catastrophic crash on the oval that cost him both legs. The circuit has since focused on DTM and national events. The oval section is rarely used today.
notable_moments: Alex Zanardi's 2001 CART accident at the oval — the crash from which he recovered to become a Paralympic cycling champion. The circuit's ambitious opening era that brought both European and American racing to eastern Germany. DTM rounds producing competitive touring car action at a circuit that rewards both mechanical grip and straightline speed.

name: Circuit de la Sarthe
bio: The world's most famous endurance race has run on these public roads and purpose-built sections since 1923. At 13.6km, it's the longest circuit in active use.
overview: The Circuit de la Sarthe is the longest circuit used in top-level motorsport and the home of the 24 Hours of Le Mans — the most prestigious endurance race in the world. At 13.626 km, it is a hybrid of closed public roads and permanent sections, including the Mulsanne Straight (a section of the RD338 departmental road), the Porsche Curves, and the Ford Chicanes. Le Mans defines endurance racing. Every manufacturer wants to win here. Every driver who does claims it as the defining achievement of their career.
history: The first 24 Hours of Le Mans was held in May 1923 on a circuit using the main road from Le Mans to Tours and a loop through local roads. The circuit evolved considerably over a century, with the Mulsanne Straight progressively tamed — two chicanes were added in 1990 after cars reached over 400 km/h on the run between Tertre Rouge and Mulsanne corner. The Porsche Curves section, the Ford Chicanes before the pit entry, and the Indianapolis and Arnage corners have all been refined over decades. The Dunlop Bridge, a landmark visible from the grandstands, has spanned the circuit since 1932. The 24 Hours was not run in 1940–48 due to the Second World War, but has taken place every year since 1949.
notable_moments: The 1955 disaster — Pierre Levegh's Mercedes disintegrated into the spectator area, killing 83 people in the deadliest accident in motorsport history. The 1966 Ford vs Ferrari battle that became the film Le Mans '66. Jacky Ickx's deliberate walk to his car in 1969, protesting the running-start format. Tom Kristensen's nine victories — the most in race history. Toyota's heartbreaking final-lap failure in 2016 when leading with minutes remaining. Ferrari's return to top-class LMH competition in 2023.

name: Circuit Bugatti
bio: The Circuit Bugatti sits entirely within the Le Mans property, using a section of the Sarthe's pit straight before looping through the infield. At 4.185 km it bears little resemblance to the 24h circuit, but the setting and history make the French MotoGP one of the calendar's most atmospheric rounds.
overview: The Circuit Bugatti is the 4.185 km permanent circuit at the Le Mans complex, used year-round for racing events separate from the famous 24 Hours. Named after Ettore Bugatti — who proposed a circuit at the site in the 1960s — it incorporates portions of the 24 Hours course (the pit straight and Dunlop sections) with a separate infield loop, making it a genuine permanent circuit in its own right. MotoGP's French Grand Prix has been held here since 2000.
history: The Circuit Bugatti was developed alongside the 24 Hours infrastructure as a way to use the Le Mans site commercially throughout the year rather than solely for one June weekend. It shares the pit complex and main straight with the 24 Hours circuit, giving MotoGP and other series access to the iconic Le Mans facilities. The MotoGP French GP's move from Paul Ricard to Le Mans brought one of the most atmospheric motorcycle events to a circuit location with unrivalled motorsport heritage. The circuit also hosts the Le Mans 24H motorcycle race for the FIM Endurance World Championship.
notable_moments: Valentino Rossi's multiple victories at the French MotoGP. Marc Marquez's emotional 2013 win in his debut MotoGP season. The annual FIM EWC 24 Hours Motos at the Bugatti circuit — a full 24-hour motorcycle endurance race. The atmosphere of racing at Le Mans, even on the shorter circuit, with the 24 Hours heritage surrounding every event.

name: London ExCeL ePrix Circuit
bio: Formula E's ExCeL Arena circuit in east London is partly indoor, running through the Royal Docks exhibition halls and out onto the surrounding streets. One of the few circuits in motorsport to be genuinely inside a building.
overview: The London ExCeL ePrix Circuit is a unique hybrid indoor/outdoor racing circuit at the ExCeL exhibition centre in the Royal Docks of east London. The Formula E layout passes through the interior of the exhibition hall — running through the building under artificial lighting — before emerging onto the outdoor dockside sections along the Royal Victoria Dock. There is no other motorsport venue in the world where racing cars run indoors as part of a world championship event.
history: Formula E introduced the London ePrix in 2015, using a temporary street circuit around the ExCeL. The indoor section was added in subsequent years, creating the unique hybrid layout. The venue reflects Formula E's strategy of bringing electric racing into urban environments and repurposed industrial spaces — the Royal Docks, once the world's largest enclosed dock system and a hub of the British Empire's trade, now hosting cutting-edge electric motorsport. The event has moved between layouts over the years as Formula E refined the London venue concept.
notable_moments: Formula E's first London ePrix in 2015 establishing electric racing in the British capital. The unique experience of racing cars entering a darkened exhibition centre — a visual unlike anything else in motorsport. London ePrix title deciders in the Formula E championship's compact, points-intensive format.

name: Streets of Long Beach
bio: California's street circuit has run through downtown Long Beach every spring since 1975. The Long Beach Grand Prix is the largest annual sporting event on the West Coast and preceded the modern era of American street racing.
overview: The Streets of Long Beach is a 3.167 km temporary street circuit through the waterfront of Long Beach, California, home of the Acura Grand Prix of Long Beach. One of the most prestigious events in American motorsport and one of North America's great street circuits, it has hosted major racing since 1975 — first F1 (1976-1983), then CART IndyCar, and the modern IndyCar series. The circuit passes through the convention centre, the waterfront hotels, and the dock area of a city built on the Port of Los Angeles's backyard.
history: Long Beach hosted Formula 1 from 1976 to 1983 as the United States Grand Prix West, a west-coast counterpart to the Watkins Glen US GP. Clay Regazzoni won the first F1 race here in 1976; Gilles Villeneuve and Alan Jones won during the race's short F1 history. After F1 departed, CART IndyCar took over and Long Beach became one of the premier events on the American open-wheel calendar. The tradition has continued unbroken — Long Beach is now one of the oldest continuously running motorsport events in the United States.
notable_moments: The 1976 inaugural F1 Long Beach GP won by Clay Regazzoni. Gilles Villeneuve's Long Beach victory. The CART IndyCar dominance eras — Al Unser Jr., Michael Andretti, Paul Tracy. Will Power's multiple IndyCar Long Beach wins. The event's extraordinary longevity as a defining American motorsport institution.

name: Lusail International Circuit
bio: Qatar's Lusail circuit was built for MotoGP in 2004 and hosts its round entirely under floodlights. Formula 1 arrived in 2021. Its high-speed flowing layout is designed for motorcycle racing and shows in the lap times.
overview: Lusail International Circuit is a 5.380 km permanent circuit in Lusail, north of Doha. Qatar's flagship motorsport venue, it hosted MotoGP from 2004 — as the championship's traditional opening night race — long before F1's arrival. Formula 1 debuted at Lusail in 2021 and has returned as a regular fixture from 2023. The circuit's long sweeping corners, smooth surface, and warm dry climate produce high-speed racing with significant tyre management demands.
history: Lusail was built in 2004 and immediately hosted the Qatar MotoGP — a night race that became the season-opening round for many years, its lit track visible against the Qatari desert a defining image of MotoGP's global calendar. Formula 1's arrival came later: a one-off 2021 race as F1 expanded its Middle Eastern calendar, followed by a long-term Qatar Grand Prix deal from 2023. The circuit sits within the Lusail development north of Doha — the same area as the Lusail Iconic Stadium that hosted the 2022 FIFA World Cup Final.
notable_moments: MotoGP's Qatar night race as a long-running season-opening tradition — riders' first competitive action under floodlights in the desert. The 2021 Qatar F1 Grand Prix debut, won by Lewis Hamilton. The 2023 Qatar GP sprint format race and Qatar's return as an F1 season fixture. Valentino Rossi's Qatar victories. The tyre failures that affected multiple cars in the 2023 Qatar F1 race, prompting post-race safety investigations.

name: Guia Street Circuit
bio: The Guia Street Circuit through Macau's colonial streets is widely considered the most dangerous circuit still in active use. Concrete barriers are centimetres from the racing line, and the 6.2km lap through the city demands absolute precision at speeds that punish any mistake permanently.
overview: The Guia Street Circuit is a 6.120 km street circuit through the Macau peninsula of the Macau Special Administrative Region. Used for the Macau Grand Prix since 1954, it is the most prestigious Formula 3 race outside of Formula 1 feeder series and one of the most dangerous circuits used in any form of top-level motorsport. The Lisboa bend — a tight right-hander following a long downhill plunge — is the most famous single corner in the circuit; the Rua do Regedor and Fisherman's Bend sections through the harbour area and mountain section that follows are equally demanding.
history: The Macau Grand Prix began in 1954 as a street race through the then-Portuguese colony. The circuit has remained essentially unchanged since — the same streets, the same buildings, the same barriers. The race grew to international significance through the Formula 3 class, which has been the headline event since the 1980s. Macau F3 became the defining proof-of-talent race for single-seater drivers on the path to Formula 1 — Ayrton Senna finished second here in 1983, Michael Schumacher won it in 1990. The FIA F3 World Cup has been the headline race in recent years. The circuit reverted to Chinese administration in 1999 with Macau's handover.
notable_moments: Michael Schumacher's 1990 victory — before his F1 debut. Ayrton Senna's second place in 1983. The 2019 race that saw multiple massive accidents including Sophia Floersch's terrifying airborne crash at Fisherman's Bend at 270 km/h — from which she recovered. Dan Ticktum's consecutive wins in 2017 and 2018. The circuit's perpetual reputation as the most demanding street circuit used in any world championship-adjacent event.

name: Jarama Circuit
bio: The Jarama circuit on the northern outskirts of Madrid hosted the Spanish Grand Prix in 1968, 1970, 1972, 1974, 1976, 1978, and 1981 before losing the race to Catalunya. Its fast, flowing layout now hosts the Formula E Madrid E-Prix.
overview: Circuit del Jarama is a 3.812 km permanent circuit 28km north of Madrid, set in the Sierra de Guadarrama foothills. A former Formula 1 venue — hosting the Spanish Grand Prix in 1968, 1970, 1972, 1974, 1976, 1978, and 1981 — it is no longer used for top-level international events but continues as a test and national motorsport venue. Its 1970s heyday coincided with the most dramatic period of Formula 1, and the circuit carries that history in its narrow, technical layout.
history: Jarama opened in 1967 and immediately entered the F1 calendar, hosting a series of Spanish GPs through the 1970s. The circuit is technical and tight — built into the scrubland hills north of Madrid with limited run-off by modern standards. It hosted Gilles Villeneuve's legendary 1981 Spanish GP win, where Villeneuve drove a slower Ferrari for an entire race but defended against four faster cars to win by less than a second — widely regarded as one of the greatest defensive drives in F1 history. Jarama was replaced on the F1 calendar by the new Circuit de Catalunya in 1991.
notable_moments: Gilles Villeneuve's 1981 Spanish GP victory — defending against a train of faster cars for lap after lap in one of F1's most celebrated drives. The circuit's 1970s grands prix featuring the era's most iconic drivers. James Hunt's 1976 Spanish GP result and subsequent disqualification controversy.

name: Madrid Circuit (Madring)
bio: The Madring — officially the Madrid Circuit — is currently under construction at the IFEMA convention complex outside Madrid. It will host the Spanish Grand Prix from 2026 onward, replacing the long-running Barcelona race.
overview: The IFEMA Madrid Circuit — known as the Madring — is a new purpose-built Formula 1 street circuit at the IFEMA Madrid exhibition and congress complex on the eastern outskirts of the Spanish capital. Announced as a new addition to the F1 calendar, it brings a second Spanish Grand Prix to the schedule alongside Barcelona, reviving Madrid's Formula 1 presence for the first time since Jarama hosted its last race in 1981.
history: The Madrid F1 Grand Prix was announced in 2023 and is being brought to the IFEMA complex — home of FITUR (the world's major tourism trade fair) and one of Europe's largest conference venues. The circuit is purpose-designed for Formula 1 around the IFEMA grounds, with the layout incorporating sections alongside and through the exhibition campus. The Madrid street circuit project represents Spain's effort to secure a long-term Formula 1 presence in the capital, complementing the established Circuit de Barcelona-Catalunya.
notable_moments: The inaugural Madrid F1 Grand Prix will establish this as a new chapter in Spanish motorsport history — a return of F1 to the capital for the first time in over four decades.

name: Circuit de Nevers Magny-Cours
bio: Nevers Magny-Cours in central France hosted the Formula 1 French Grand Prix from 1991 to 2008. Its remote location contributed to its eventual removal from the calendar, but its technical layout remains a regular fixture in the ELMS and WSBK calendars.
overview: The Circuit de Nevers Magny-Cours is a 4.411 km circuit in Burgundy, central France, developed from a minor national circuit into an F1 venue through a 1980s government investment programme. Remote by European motorsport standards — far from any major city — it hosted the French Grand Prix from 1991 to 2008 before F1's departure. Today it hosts WEC and ELMS rounds, and retains a faithful following in French motorsport despite its geography.
history: Magny-Cours was upgraded to F1 standard as part of a regional employment initiative under President François Mitterrand in the late 1980s. It hosted the French GP from 1991 to 2008. The circuit was controversial within F1 — remote, difficult to access, lacking the infrastructure of more urban venues — and its loss of the French GP reflected these pressures. Since then the circuit has focused on WEC, ELMS, and national championship rounds, maintaining its facilities without major further investment.
notable_moments: Michael Schumacher's multiple victories during his Ferrari dominance years. The 2008 French Grand Prix — the last F1 race at the circuit. The Adelaide hairpin, one of the most satisfying long right-handers in European motorsport. The circuit's quiet, almost private atmosphere compared to the spectacle of larger venues.

name: Pertamina Mandalika Street Circuit
bio: The Pertamina Mandalika Street Circuit on the island of Lombok opened for MotoGP in 2022. Its coastal setting and capacity for 150,000 spectators make it one of the most ambitious venues added to the calendar in decades.
overview: Pertamina Mandalika Street Circuit is a 4.319 km permanent street circuit on the southern coast of Lombok island, Indonesia. Built within the Mandalika tourism development zone — a government-backed resort project on Lombok's white-sand coast — it hosts MotoGP and WorldSBK rounds in a tropical island setting unprecedented in world motorsport. The circuit runs through the Mandalika resort development with the Indian Ocean visible from parts of the track, combining infrastructure-grade racing facilities with a holiday island landscape.
history: Mandalika was built as part of Indonesia's ambition to develop Lombok as a premium tourism destination while simultaneously returning Indonesia to the world motorsport calendar. The circuit was fast-tracked for construction and first hosted a WorldSBK round in 2021 and MotoGP in 2022. The surface quality in the first seasons was problematic — a rubber compound used in the track surface degraded rapidly, creating grip inconsistencies — but these issues were progressively addressed. The Pertamina sponsorship naming reflects Indonesia's state oil company's investment in the sport.
notable_moments: MotoGP's return to Indonesia after over two decades — the country's last premier class motorcycle race had been held at Sentul in 1997. WorldSBK's inaugural Mandalika round in 2021. The circuit's extraordinary setting on a tropical island making it the most visually distinctive venue on either the MotoGP or WorldSBK calendar.

name: Streets of Markham
bio: The Streets of Markham circuit north of Toronto is one of IndyCar's newest venues. Canada's second IndyCar street circuit after Long Beach, it reflects the series' growing presence in major North American cities.
overview: Streets of Markham is a temporary street circuit in Markham, Ontario — a city immediately north of Toronto in the Greater Toronto Area. One of IndyCar's newer additions to the calendar, it brings top-level open-wheel racing to Canada's largest metropolitan market for the first time since Toronto's street race ceased operations. Markham's identity as one of Canada's most diverse and fastest-growing suburban cities provides the urban energy for a street circuit event.
history: Streets of Markham joined the IndyCar calendar as part of the series' return to Toronto-area racing after the longstanding Honda Indy Toronto event at Exhibition Place ceased. Markham's position in the GTA, with its large automotive industry workforce and significant motorsport fanbase drawn from Toronto and surrounding areas, made it an attractive location for an IndyCar street event. The circuit represents IndyCar's ongoing Canadian market development strategy.
notable_moments: IndyCar's return to the Toronto metropolitan area through the Markham street circuit. The event's significance for Canadian motorsport — bringing the IndyCar series back to Canada's largest urban market.

name: Martinsville Speedway
bio: America's oldest NASCAR oval still in use, the Martinsville paperclip has been racing since 1947. At 0.526 miles, its flat corners and straightaways are a bumper-to-bumper short-track exercise in patience and aggression.
overview: Martinsville Speedway is a 0.526-mile paperclip oval in Martinsville, Virginia — the oldest track on the NASCAR Cup schedule, having hosted races since 1947. The flat, concrete-and-asphalt hybrid surface and the extremely tight paper clip shape make Martinsville the most physically demanding short track in the series: heavy braking into hairpin-like corners, hard acceleration down the short straights, and constant door-to-door contact characterise every race. The winner receives a grandfather clock trophy — one of motorsport's most distinctive awards.
history: Martinsville was built by H. Clay Earles in 1947 and has hosted NASCAR races since 1949 — every year of the modern Cup era without interruption. The circuit's shape has changed little in 75 years. It was originally unpaved and ran without lights; today it is a fully modern facility with lighting and concrete apron. NASCAR's Martinsville events have been among the series' most consistently competitive and physical, with the short, slow circuit producing multiple lead changes and incidents in a way that faster tracks cannot.
notable_moments: Richard Petty's 15 victories — the record at any single venue. Darrell Waltrip's multiple wins. The grandfather clock tradition. The controversial 2021 fall race where Brad Keselowski wrecked Martin Truex Jr. — a collision with championship implications. The circuit's heritage as the oldest venue in continuous NASCAR use.

name: Autodromo Hermanos Rodríguez
bio: The Autodromo Hermanos Rodriguez sits 2,250 metres above sea level in Mexico City, making it the highest circuit on any World Championship calendar. The legendary Peraltada banked final corner and the Foro Sol stadium section are its defining features.
overview: Autodromo Hermanos Rodríguez is a 4.304 km permanent circuit in the Magdalena Mixhuca sports complex of Mexico City, named after brothers Ricardo and Pedro Rodríguez — Mexico's first great racing drivers, both killed in racing accidents. Situated at 2,240 metres above sea level, it is the highest circuit on the Formula 1 calendar: the thin air reduces aerodynamic downforce and engine output while making power units work harder on cooling. The Peraltada — the iconic final sweeping corner — was modified in the 1990s when a baseball stadium was built in its centre, creating the current stadium section.
history: The circuit hosted its first Mexican Grand Prix in 1963. Ricardo Rodríguez died at the circuit during qualifying for that 1962 pre-championship race at age 20, and his brother Pedro died at the Norisring in 1971 — both before they could fulfil their potential. The circuit hosted F1 from 1963 to 1970 and again from 1986 to 1992, returning in 2015 under a new long-term contract. The stadium section, where 40,000 fans pack the arena built into the original Peraltada, creates one of the most atmospheric settings in Formula 1.
notable_moments: Ricardo Rodríguez's death at the circuit in 1962. The 1990 Mexican GP where Gerhard Berger won. The return of the Mexican GP in 2015 and its immediate status as one of the best-attended races on the calendar. The passionate Mexican crowd — particularly for Sergio Perez's home race — creating an atmosphere comparable to Monza's Tifosi. Max Verstappen's multiple Mexico City victories.

name: Miami International Autodrome
bio: The Hard Rock Stadium circuit debuted in 2022 and encircles the NFL venue in Miami Gardens. An artificial marina, long straights, and an enclosed stadium section give it a very American spectacle feel distinct from traditional F1 venues.
overview: Miami International Autodrome is a 5.412 km semi-permanent circuit built around Hard Rock Stadium — home of the Miami Dolphins NFL team — in Miami Gardens, Florida. Opened in 2022, it hosts the Miami Grand Prix as one of two US F1 races (alongside COTA). The circuit uses the stadium's massive parking lots and surrounding roads, with a distinctive section passing through an artificial marina environment constructed adjacent to the stadium. The Miami GP has positioned itself as one of F1's most glamorous events, mirroring the city's luxury brand image.
history: Miami joined the F1 calendar in 2022 after years of negotiations between Formula One Management, Miami-Dade County, and Hard Rock Stadium's operators. Multiple proposed layouts were considered before the stadium-encircling route was confirmed. The circuit was constructed on what is essentially a permanent parking lot surface, requiring significant new paving. The event has emphasised the lifestyle and celebrity dimensions of Formula 1's American expansion, with elaborate hospitality zones and high-profile attendance.
notable_moments: The inaugural 2022 Miami Grand Prix won by Max Verstappen. The 2023 race won by Verstappen from a sprint race weekend format. The event's establishment as one of F1's most commercially successful new additions. The artificial marina backdrop that generated significant commentary for its theatrical approach to circuit design.

name: Michigan International Speedway
bio: Michigan International Speedway's two-mile high-banked oval produces the fastest average lap speeds in NASCAR, frequently exceeding 200mph. Drafting and aerodynamic positioning are the primary tools for gaining position.
overview: Michigan International Speedway is a 2.0-mile D-shaped superspeedway in Brooklyn, Michigan — one of the fastest tracks in NASCAR by average speed. The long, wide oval with relatively gentle banking produces sustained high-speed racing where aerodynamic efficiency dominates. Michigan has historically been one of the places where NASCAR's fastest cars are revealed, with qualifying and race speeds regularly among the highest of the season.
history: Michigan opened in 1968 and has hosted NASCAR Cup races since. The circuit was designed by Charles Moneypenny, who intended a high-speed venue capable of producing record lap speeds. It largely succeeded — Michigan has been one of NASCAR's fastest ovals across its entire history. IndyCar also held events at Michigan's oval for many years, with the high-banked oval producing oval speeds that challenged road course-focused modern cars.
notable_moments: David Pearson's and Richard Petty's Michigan battles in the 1970s. Mark Martin's multiple Michigan victories over decades of NASCAR racing. The oval's reputation as a circuit where raw horsepower matters more than anywhere else in NASCAR — making it a bellwether for manufacturer performance.

name: Mid-Ohio Sports Car Course
bio: Mid-Ohio Sports Car Course opened in 1962, carved through natural Ohio terrain. Its narrow, twisty layout has no room for error and rewards circuit knowledge accumulated over many laps.
overview: Mid-Ohio Sports Car Course is a 3.746 km permanent road course in the rolling farmland of north-central Ohio, near Lexington. One of America's classic road courses, Mid-Ohio has a technical, flowing character that rewards smooth, precise driving — the Carousel and the Keyhole are demanding corners that differentiate the skilled from the simply fast. IndyCar's Honda Indy 200 at Mid-Ohio is a regular season fixture, and IMSA also uses the circuit.
history: Mid-Ohio opened in 1962 and immediately attracted major road racing events. The circuit has been continuously used for over six decades, hosting Can-Am, Trans-Am, CART IndyCar, and the modern IndyCar series. Its proximity to Ohio's population centres and the region's strong motorsport culture have kept it on major American racing calendars. The circuit has changed relatively little from its original concept — a flowing, demanding road course in agricultural Ohio.
notable_moments: John Andretti's IndyCar victories at Mid-Ohio. Consecutive IndyCar championships decided or shaped by Mid-Ohio results. The Honda relationship with the circuit — Honda's IndyCar engine partner maintains deep connections with an Ohio venue that serves as a showcase.

name: The Milwaukee Mile
bio: The Milwaukee Mile is America's oldest operating motor racing venue, having held races since 1903. The one-mile oval is fast, flat, and brutally simple — a pure test of oval racing craft.
overview: The Milwaukee Mile is a 1.0-mile oval at the Wisconsin State Fair Park in West Allis, Wisconsin, considered the oldest active motorsport venue in the world, having hosted races since 1903. A paved oval on the State Fair grounds, The Milwaukee Mile has hosted IndyCar racing across multiple eras of the sport's history — from the original American Automobile Association days through USAC, CART, and the modern IndyCar series. It has been in and out of major championship calendars but retains its status as American motorsport's most historic venue.
history: The Milwaukee Mile began as a horse racing track at the Wisconsin State Fair in 1876. Automobiles first raced here in 1903, making it the oldest motorised racing venue still in use. The track has hosted IndyCar racing in virtually every era of the sport, with only gaps when series schedules changed. The State Fair Park setting means the track is embedded in an agricultural fair infrastructure that gives it a character entirely unlike any purpose-built motorsport facility — grandstands built for a county fair rather than a race crowd.
notable_moments: Over 120 years of American motorsport history at a single venue. A.J. Foyt's victories at Milwaukee across multiple decades. The track's appearances in IndyCar's modern era as an occasional calendar addition reviving the historic venue. The State Fair context — racing on the same grounds as livestock competitions and agricultural exhibits since 1876.

name: Misano World Circuit Marco Simoncelli
bio: The Marco Simoncelli World Circuit on the Adriatic Riviera runs clockwise — unusual in MotoGP. It hosts both MotoGP's San Marino round and WSBK, and carries the name of the Italian rider lost there in 2011.
overview: The Misano World Circuit Marco Simoncelli is a 4.226 km circuit near Rimini on the Adriatic coast of Italy. Renamed in 2012 in memory of Marco Simoncelli — the Italian MotoGP rider from nearby Coriano who died at the Malaysian Grand Prix in 2011 — the circuit is one of the most multi-discipline venues on the European calendar, hosting MotoGP, WorldSBK, and DTM rounds. Simoncelli's face and number 58 are woven into the circuit's identity.
history: Misano opened in 1972 as the Circuito Internazionale di Misano. It hosted MotoGP from 2007 onwards as a second Italian round separate from Mugello. The circuit was rebuilt and expanded in 2007 and 2012. Marco Simoncelli, who grew up in Coriano just minutes from the circuit, was a beloved figure in Italian motorsport — fast, extrovert, fearless. His death at the 2011 Malaysian GP sent shockwaves through the sport. The renaming of the circuit was a fitting tribute to a rider whose personality matched the passionate region he came from.
notable_moments: Marco Simoncelli's victories at his home circuit before his death in 2011. The annual emotional tribute at the renamed circuit — a race weekend infused with memory for Italian fans. Consistent WorldSBK title battles at Misano, which frequently hosts mid-season rounds when the championship is at a decisive point.

name: Circuit de Monaco
bio: The Principality's street circuit has barely changed since 1929. Barely wide enough for two cars, utterly unforgiving, and the one race every driver wants to win.
overview: The Circuit de Monaco is 3.337 km of public streets through the principality, winding through tunnels, hairpins, and barriers with zero margin for error. It is the slowest circuit on the Formula 1 calendar by average speed, the most prestigious race in the world by reputation, and — by driver consensus — the most technically demanding single lap in motorsport. The barriers are everywhere. A moment's error eliminates you. Nothing tests car control and concentration like Monaco.
history: Monaco has hosted a Grand Prix since 1929. The route through the principality — the harbour tunnel, the Mirabeau hairpin, the Rascasse, the swimming pool — has remained fundamentally unchanged since the post-war era. F1 first visited in 1950 and Monaco has been a permanent fixture since 1955. The circuit is rebuilt each year on public roads, which are otherwise used by normal traffic the rest of the year. The aquatic centre beneath the harbour section was built in the 1980s, raising the pool section above the water level.
notable_moments: Ayrton Senna's six Monaco victories — a record that became the measure of greatness. His 1984 race leading in the rain before the red flag ended it with him far ahead. The 1996 race where only three cars finished. Sergio Perez's controversial last-lap overtake on Charles Leclerc in 2022. The timeless image of Nelson Piquet's shunt at the Loews hairpin. Every year, watching millionaire yachts bob in the harbour while drivers millimetres from the armco.

name: Autodromo Nazionale di Monza
bio: Italy's Temple of Speed has hosted the Italian Grand Prix every year since 1950. Long straights and minimal downforce make it the fastest lap on the F1 calendar.
overview: Monza is the fastest circuit on the Formula 1 calendar and the oldest purpose-built racing circuit still in active use at the top level of the sport. Its 5.793 km layout — long straights, tight chicanes, virtually no medium-speed corners — produces the highest average speeds in F1 and consistently the lowest lap times of the season. Set within the Royal Park of Monza north of Milan, the circuit is surrounded by trees and the ruins of its original concrete banking, which was used until 1969 and still stands today. The Italian crowd — the Tifosi — are among the most passionate in motorsport.
history: Monza opened in 1922, just 110 days after construction began, making it one of the fastest circuit builds in history. The original layout included a road course and a banked oval; combined layouts using both sections were used until 1969, when the banking was deemed too dangerous after accidents. The modern Grand Prix circuit is road-course only, with chicanes inserted at Variante del Rettifilo, Variante della Roggia, and Variante Ascari following fatal accidents in the 1960s and 1970s. Monza has hosted the Italian Grand Prix every year except 1980 (held at Imola that year) — making it the most consistently used venue in F1 history.
notable_moments: The 1971 Italian Grand Prix — a five-car slipstream fight to the line, decided by 0.01 seconds as Peter Gethin won from Ronnie Peterson. The 2020 Italian GP, won by Pierre Gasly for AlphaTauri after Lewis Hamilton received a pitlane penalty — one of F1's most unexpected results of the modern era. The annual ritual of the Tifosi storming the track after the race. Sebastian Vettel's multiple victories here driving for Ferrari.

name: Circuit Gilles Villeneuve
bio: Île Notre-Dame's circuit was built on land reclaimed for Expo 67. Its Wall of Champions has claimed more careers than most, and the Casino hairpin produces some of the most dramatic late-braking duels in the sport.
overview: Circuit Gilles Villeneuve is a 4.361 km semi-permanent street circuit on the Île Notre-Dame in the Saint Lawrence River, Montreal. Named after Gilles Villeneuve — the beloved Canadian racing driver who died in qualifying at Zolder in 1982 — the circuit is one of F1's most popular events and most dramatic venues. Low-speed chicanes connected by very fast straights produce heavy braking zones and heavy contact between drivers who run at the limit of overtaking possibility all race. The Wall of Champions at the final chicane has collected countless championship contenders over the years.
history: The Circuit Gilles Villeneuve hosted its first Canadian Grand Prix in 1978. Following Villeneuve's death in 1982, the circuit was renamed in his honour. The circuit is built on infrastructure from Expo 67 and the 1976 Olympic Games, making Île Notre-Dame one of the most significant urban heritage sites in Canada. Formula E has also used the circuit, and the race weekend is one of Montreal's biggest annual events, bringing hundreds of thousands of visitors to the island each June.
notable_moments: Gilles Villeneuve's victories here in front of his home crowd. The Wall of Champions — Michael Schumacher, Damon Hill, and Jacques Villeneuve all crashing at the final chicane in consecutive years (1999). Jenson Button's extraordinary 2011 Canadian GP — a race red-flagged for two hours of rain, restarted, and decided in the final lap as Button overcame a drive-through penalty to win. Lewis Hamilton's multiple Montreal victories. The circuit's reputation as one of the best races on the F1 calendar.

name: Canadian Tire Motorsport Park
bio: Canadian Tire Motorsport Park opened in 1961 in hilly Ontario countryside. Its long, undulating lap and fast corners have made it one of the most respected — and unforgiving — circuits in North America.
overview: Canadian Tire Motorsport Park — historically known as Mosport — is a 3.957 km permanent road course in the countryside east of Toronto, Ontario. One of Canada's great road circuits, it is fast, flowing, and demanding, with significant elevation changes and corners that require full commitment. The circuit has hosted Canadian GP events in F1's history and remains a fixture for IndyCar and IMSA, producing close racing on a layout that genuinely tests cars and drivers.
history: Mosport opened in 1961 and hosted the Canadian Grand Prix in F1 in 1967, 1969, 1971, 1972, 1973, and 1977 before the race moved to Circuit Gilles Villeneuve in Montreal. The circuit has a rich history in Can-Am, Trans-Am, and international sports car racing from the 1960s and 1970s. Corporate naming rights changed the official name to Canadian Tire Motorsport Park, but the Mosport identity has never left — it remains how most motorsport fans refer to it. IndyCar uses the circuit as one of its Canadian rounds.
notable_moments: The 1973 Canadian GP won by Peter Revson. Can-Am era battles between McLarens and Lolas in the late 1960s. IndyCar rounds at Mosport in the modern era. The circuit's longevity as one of North America's most respected permanent road course facilities.

name: Autodrom Most
bio: Autodrom Most in the Czech Republic is one of the newest facilities regularly used in DTM. Its tight, technical layout in the Bohemian countryside joined the premier touring car calendar in 2021.
overview: Autodrom Most is a 4.212 km permanent circuit in the industrial town of Most in northwest Bohemia, Czech Republic. Built literally on top of a former open-pit lignite coal mine that was filled in and landscaped, the circuit sits on reclaimed industrial land — one of the most unusual foundations of any racing venue in Europe. It joined the DTM calendar in 2021 and has established itself as a consistent fixture in German touring car racing.
history: The original Autodrom Most opened in 1983 during the communist era, making it one of the more prominent motorsport venues in Eastern European racing. The circuit hosted various national and Central European events through the 1980s and 1990s. It was rebuilt and modernised in the 2000s and 2010s. The DTM's expansion of its calendar to include more Central and Eastern European venues brought Most onto the international calendar in 2021. The circuit sits within the North Bohemian Basin, an area historically dominated by coal extraction, giving it a distinctly industrial backdrop.
notable_moments: Most's first DTM round in 2021, marking the circuit's arrival on the international stage. The circuit's unique geology — racing on filled mining land — has no equivalent in European motorsport. ELMS endurance rounds at the circuit have produced close multi-class competition.

name: Mobility Resort Motegi
bio: Honda's home circuit in the Tochigi hills has a distinctive mix of the original oval section and the technical infield. It hosts MotoGP's Japanese round and is one of the sport's most important commercial weekends.
overview: Mobility Resort Motegi (formerly Twin Ring Motegi) is a 4.801 km circuit in Tochigi Prefecture, Japan, owned by Honda. It is the home of the Japanese MotoGP and a regular WEC venue, and forms the centrepiece of Honda's Mobility Resort complex — a sprawling automotive themed park that includes a motorsport museum, driving experiences, and Honda vehicle displays. The circuit has a distinctive teardrop-shaped oval alongside the road course, though the oval is now rarely used for top-level events.
history: Twin Ring Motegi was built by Honda and opened in 1997, designed to host both oval racing (bringing American IndyCar events to Japan) and road course events. The IndyCar oval race ran here through the late 1990s and early 2000s. MotoGP adopted Motegi as the Japanese GP venue, and it has been the home of that race since 1999. Honda's ownership means every major Honda motorsport milestone — world championship titles, new bike launches — is celebrated here. The circuit was renamed Mobility Resort Motegi in 2022 as part of Honda's broader sustainability and mobility rebranding.
notable_moments: Multiple MotoGP Japanese GP title deciders at Motegi. The CART IndyCar oval events of the early 2000s bringing American open-wheel racing to a Japanese audience. Honda's championship celebration events at the circuit they own. WEC rounds at Motegi as part of the championship's Asian leg.

name: Autodromo del Mugello
bio: Owned by Ferrari and tucked into the Tuscan Apennines, Mugello's high-speed flowing layout is widely considered the most beautiful permanent circuit in Europe.
overview: The Mugello Circuit is a 5.245 km circuit owned by Ferrari, set in the Tuscan hills north of Florence. Ferrari uses it as their primary test facility for F1 cars between races — every new Ferrari turns its first laps here. MotoGP's Italian GP has been held at Mugello since 1994. The circuit is fast and flowing, with long corners and dramatic elevation changes, and the back straight — the Rettifilo — is the fastest section of any MotoGP circuit, where bikes regularly exceed 350 km/h.
history: Motorsport in the Mugello valley dates back to road races in the 1920s using the valley roads. The current permanent circuit was built in 1974 and Ferrari purchased it in 1988. The circuit has been continuously developed since, with modern pits, grandstands, and run-off areas added while retaining the sweeping original layout through the hillsides. Mugello's role as Ferrari's private test circuit means access is tightly controlled outside race weekends — making its race events particularly atmospheric for the Ferrari-devoted Italian crowd.
notable_moments: Valentino Rossi's legendary MotoGP victories here, his de facto home race with supporters from nearby Tavullia filling the Mugello hillsides. The 2020 Tuscan Grand Prix — Formula 1's first world championship race at Mugello since 1994 — won by Lewis Hamilton with Ferrari's 1000th GP milestone as the backdrop. Marco Simoncelli's final Italian GP podium in 2011, months before his death in Malaysia.

name: Nashville Street Circuit
bio: The Music City Grand Prix street circuit winds through downtown Nashville along the Cumberland River, with Nissan Stadium as its backdrop. Launched in 2021, it quickly became one of IndyCar's most distinctive events, blending urban scenery with fast, flowing tarmac.
overview: The Nashville Street Circuit is a 3.445 km temporary street circuit in Nashville, Tennessee, running around and across the Cumberland River via the Korean Veterans Memorial Bridge. The distinctive circuit passes through the Nissan Stadium area and crosses a bridge during the lap — one of the few circuits in world motorsport where the racing surface spans a river. IndyCar's Big Machine Music City Grand Prix uses the circuit, connecting motorsport with Nashville's entertainment identity.
history: The Nashville street circuit debuted in 2021 for IndyCar's Big Machine Music City Grand Prix, bringing top-level open-wheel racing back to Nashville for the first time in decades. Formula E also added Nashville to its calendar. The circuit's bridge section — requiring extensive safety infrastructure to allow racing on a public highway bridge — was a significant engineering challenge that became the circuit's defining characteristic. Nashville's cultural profile as "Music City" has made the race weekend a music and entertainment festival as much as a motorsport event.
notable_moments: IndyCar's inaugural Nashville Grand Prix in 2021 producing a dramatic race in the Tennessee summer heat. Marcus Ericsson's victory. The bridge section's visual appeal becoming the most-shared image from the event globally. The race's integration with Nashville's broader entertainment identity.

name: Nashville Superspeedway
bio: Nashville Superspeedway is a 1.33-mile concrete D-oval east of Nashville in Lebanon, Tennessee. Opened in 2001, it sat dormant from 2008 until NASCAR returned in 2021. Its concrete surface and tight D-shape produce close, physical racing with heavy tire wear.
overview: Nashville Superspeedway is a 1.333-mile concrete oval in Lebanon, Tennessee — 30 miles east of Nashville. The smooth, steeply banked concrete oval sat dormant for many years before NASCAR Cup racing returned to it in 2021. Its concrete surface distinguishes it from most modern NASCAR ovals, creating a different racing dynamic and tyre degradation pattern that rewards drivers and engineers who understand the material.
history: Nashville Superspeedway opened in 2001 and hosted NASCAR's Nationwide (now Xfinity) and Craftsman (now Truck) Series through 2011 before sitting unused for a decade. The oval was brought back for NASCAR Cup in 2021 as part of the series' schedule diversification efforts, adding another concrete oval alongside Bristol and Dover. The facility is maintained throughout dormancy and the concrete surface returned to competition in good condition.
notable_moments: NASCAR Cup's return to Nashville Superspeedway in 2021 — the first Cup race at the venue since 1984 when it used a different format. Kyle Larson's dominant victory in the inaugural Cup event. The concrete oval's distinctive racing character differentiating it from the asphalt ovals that dominate the Cup schedule.

name: New Hampshire Motor Speedway
bio: New Hampshire Motor Speedway's Magic Mile is a flat, one-mile oval in Loudon. Precision braking and late-apex corner entry are the defining skills — subtle differences in entry and exit angles separate results.
overview: New Hampshire Motor Speedway is a 1.058-mile oval in Loudon, New Hampshire — The Magic Mile. One of the tighter intermediate ovals on the NASCAR Cup schedule, it produces close racing on a relatively flat surface where mechanical grip and tyre management dominate. Located in the northeast, New Hampshire serves a motorsport audience in a region not typically associated with NASCAR, drawing from across New England.
history: New Hampshire Motor Speedway opened in 1990 and joined the NASCAR Cup schedule in 1993. It has historically hosted two Cup races per season, making it one of the more frequently visited venues on the northern calendar. The circuit's flat, tight character produces a different racing product from the high-banked southern ovals — more mechanical, less aerodynamic. The NH Motor Speedway audience represents NASCAR's northernmost core fanbase.
notable_moments: Jeff Burton's multiple New Hampshire victories. Jimmie Johnson's wins here during his championship runs. The circuit's role as NASCAR's northeastern flagship event drawing fans from Maine, Vermont, Massachusetts, and beyond.

name: Norisring
bio: The Norisring runs around the former Nazi Party rally grounds in Nuremberg city centre. At 2.3km it's the shortest circuit in DTM, and its tight hairpin combined with the long Schöller straight produces one of the most intense overtaking spots in German motorsport.
overview: The Norisring is a 2.300 km street circuit in the city of Nuremberg, Bavaria, constructed around the Dutzendteich lake and using public roads adjacent to the Nazi-era Zeppelin Field. The shortest circuit on the DTM calendar and one of the shortest used in any major touring car championship, it is defined by two long straights connected by tight hairpins — producing intense slipstream battles and spectacular overtaking around the lakeside parkland. The Norisring is one of DTM's most distinctive and atmospheric events.
history: The Norisring has hosted motorsport since 1947 and has been a fixture on the DTM calendar for most of the championship's history. The circuit uses roads that are otherwise used by Nuremberg's traffic, closed for race weekend. The venue's proximity to the former Reichsparteitagsgelände — the Nazi congress grounds — gives it an unavoidable historical weight; the Zeppelin Field grandstand is visible from the circuit. The Norisring's compact layout and lakeside setting make it unique among European street circuits.
notable_moments: DTM battles at the Norisring producing some of the championship's most dramatic moments — the short, tight circuit amplifying contact and tactical decisions. The circuit's historical surroundings in Nuremberg. The slipstream battles down the two long straights that effectively determine race outcomes.

name: Nürburgring Nordschleife
bio: Twenty kilometres, 154 corners, and no run-off. The Nordschleife has been the world's definitive proving ground since 1927 — Jackie Stewart called it the Green Hell.
overview: The Nordschleife is 20.832 km of forested road circuit built in 1927, carved through the Eifel hills of western Germany. Jackie Stewart called it The Green Hell. It has 73 corners, 300 metres of elevation change, and conditions that vary dramatically from one end of the circuit to the other. Nothing else in motorsport compares to it. The NLS (Nürburgring Langstrecken-Serie) runs here year-round, combining 200+ cars across dozens of classes in endurance races that attract professional factory teams and enthusiast club racers sharing the same circuit simultaneously.
history: The Nordschleife was built between 1925 and 1927 as a public works project providing employment for 3,000 workers during Germany's post-war economic depression. It hosted the German Grand Prix from 1927 until Niki Lauda's near-fatal fire in 1976 — the race he returned to six weeks later at Monza, forever defining his legend. Formula 1 never returned to the Nordschleife after 1976. The circuit has been used continuously since for the NLS, the 24H Nürburgring (which combines the Nordschleife with the GP circuit), touring car racing, and as a manufacturer test track.
notable_moments: Niki Lauda's 1976 accident and survival. Stefan Bellof's 6:11.13 lap record in 1983 — still the fastest racing lap of the full circuit. The annual 24H Nürburgring, one of the most chaotic and atmospheric endurance races in the world. The NLS culture that puts factory Porsches, BMWs, and Ferraris on track alongside privately-entered Renault Clios and Mazda MX-5s.

name: North Wilkesboro Speedway
bio: North Wilkesboro Speedway is a historic North Carolina short track that closed in 1996. Revived in 2023 for the All-Star Race, it returned racing to one of NASCAR's most significant original venues.
overview: North Wilkesboro Speedway is a 0.625-mile oval in North Wilkesboro, North Carolina — one of the original NASCAR tracks, built in 1947 and closed in 1996. The track's resurrection as the host of the NASCAR All-Star Race from 2023 gave it a new identity as a heritage venue repurposed for the sport's showcase non-points event. The short oval's character — narrow, rough in places, demanding in its own right — connects modern NASCAR with its Appalachian mountain roots.
history: North Wilkesboro was one of the original tracks on the NASCAR Grand National circuit, built on farmland in the North Carolina foothills in 1947. It hosted Cup races continuously until 1996, when NASCAR redistributed its dates to new markets — the tracks in Charlotte and Texas received the North Wilkesboro slots. The facility sat dormant and slowly decayed for over two decades. Its revival for the 2023 All-Star Race required significant renovation but retained the track's historic character.
notable_moments: Richard Petty's and Cale Yarborough's early-era victories that built the track's legend. The 1996 final race — the end of an era for a venue central to NASCAR's Appalachian heritage. The 2023 All-Star Race revival — a homecoming for NASCAR to one of its defining original venues.

name: Nürburgring GP Circuit
bio: The modern GP circuit sits alongside the Nordschleife and hosts the German rounds of the DTM and NLS endurance series. Compact but technical, it punishes mistakes without mercy.
overview: The Nürburgring GP-Strecke is a modern 5.148 km FIA-grade circuit built in 1984 alongside the legendary Nordschleife. It hosts WEC, ELMS, Formula E, and DTM rounds, and shares pit facilities with the Nordschleife for the combined 24H Nürburgring race. Where the Nordschleife is raw and historic, the GP circuit is smooth, technically precise, and built to the safety standards of the modern era. It sits in the same Eifel landscape — surrounded by forest and hill — and carries the same unpredictable weather.
history: The GP circuit was built after Formula 1 left the Nordschleife following Niki Lauda's near-fatal accident in 1976. The new circuit opened in 1984 and hosted the European Grand Prix that year. It has been used by F1, DTM, WEC, and Formula E at various points. The Nürburgring site went bankrupt in 2012 after a disastrous expansion that included a theme park and indoor ski slope; the complex was purchased and rebuilt, with the GP circuit continuing as the primary FIA-grade venue while the Nordschleife focuses on the endurance calendar.
notable_moments: The 1999 European Grand Prix where Eddie Irvine pushed Michael Schumacher for the championship, and Michael came out of retirement as a Ferrari substitute. The 2020 Eifel Grand Prix — F1's first visit since 2013 — run in cold, foggy conditions that saw the medical helicopter grounded and the race delayed. Formula E's multiple ePrix events in the forested Eifel setting.

name: Motorsport Arena Oschersleben
bio: Motorsport Arena Oschersleben in Saxony-Anhalt is one of Germany's few permanent road circuits. A regular DTM and European championship venue, its tight infield section and long back straight produce varied racing.
overview: Motorsport Arena Oschersleben is a 3.696 km permanent circuit near the city of Oschersleben in Saxony-Anhalt, eastern Germany. A consistent fixture on the DTM calendar, it also hosts national championship events and has previously been used for WTCC rounds. The circuit is flat and technical, with good overtaking opportunities and a modern pit complex, sitting in the agricultural lowlands of central Germany.
history: Oschersleben opened in 1997 and quickly established itself as a DTM venue. Its position in the former East Germany made it part of the expansion of international motorsport into the eastern German states following reunification. The circuit has hosted DTM rounds consistently, providing a central German location on the touring car calendar that alternates with Hockenheim, Most, and the Norisring. WTCC used Oschersleben as an early fixture when the series established its European calendar.
notable_moments: DTM championship rounds at Oschersleben contributing to multiple touring car title battles. The circuit's role in establishing Saxony-Anhalt as a motorsport destination in unified Germany.

name: Oulton Park
bio: Set in a Cheshire deer park with dramatic elevation changes and flowing corners, Oulton Park is widely considered the most beautiful permanent circuit in the UK. Its technical layout rewards driver skill over raw machinery.
overview: Oulton Park is a 2.692 km (Island circuit) permanent circuit in Little Budworth, Cheshire, set in parkland with a lake and mature trees. One of the most naturally scenic circuits in Britain, it hosts BTCC and British Superbike Championship rounds, and its combination of fast flowing sections and technical changes of direction makes it a genuine driver's circuit. The undulating Cheshire landscape gives Oulton's corners genuine elevation change that produces unusual handling demands.
history: Oulton Park opened in 1953 on the estate of Oulton Park Hall, using the natural topography of the parkland. It has hosted British motorsport events continuously since, becoming an established BTCC and BSB venue. The circuit is owned by Motorsport Vision, which also operates Brands Hatch, Donington Park, and Snetterton. The Island circuit uses the core loop; the International layout adds the longer Cascades section for certain events.
notable_moments: BTCC rounds at Oulton Park consistently producing close, unpredictable racing given the circuit's technical character. British Superbike battles through the parkland setting. The circuit's visual beauty — trees, lake, rolling Cheshire countryside — making it one of the most pleasant venues on the British calendar for spectators.

name: Circuit Paul Ricard
bio: Circuit Paul Ricard on the Castellet plateau above Marseille is famous for its blue-painted asphalt run-offs that eliminate gravel traps entirely. It served as F1's French GP venue from 2018 to 2022 and remains the primary European test circuit.
overview: The Circuit Paul Ricard in Le Castellet, Provence is a 5.842 km circuit (Grand Prix configuration) owned by Formula One Management. Its most distinctive feature is the coloured Mistral Club asphalt: different coloured tarmac zones in the run-off areas that function as a braking analysis tool — engineers can see precisely where a car leaves the track based on which colour stripe it reaches. Wide, smooth, forgiving, and sun-baked, Ricard is a test track at heart, and the atmosphere reflects it.
history: Paul Ricard was built by pastis entrepreneur Paul Ricard on his land in the Var département in 1969-1970, and immediately attracted attention for its unusually wide run-off areas — a radical safety concept at the time. F1 used the circuit from 1971 to 1990. The circuit was purchased by Bernie Ecclestone and Formula One Management in 1999, and a major rebuild followed, installing the distinctive coloured asphalt. F1 returned from 2018 to 2022. Ricard has consistently hosted WEC and ELMS rounds, and functions as the primary pre-season test venue for numerous series due to its consistent climate and driver-friendly layout.
notable_moments: Didier Pironi's career-ending accident during 1982 German GP practice. Elio de Angelis's fatal testing accident at the circuit in 1986 — an event that accelerated changes to how circuit medical services operated. The multiple WEC rounds that have produced close endurance battles on the long Mistral Straight. The circuit's role as the most-used test venue in European motorsport.

name: Wanneroo Raceway
bio: Wanneroo Raceway in Western Australia is the sole permanent circuit in the west of the country. Its smooth surface and multiple configurations provide a regular stop for national-level Australian motorsport.
overview: Wanneroo Raceway — officially Barbagallo Wanneroo Raceway — is a 3.430 km permanent circuit in Neerabup, 30km north of Perth's CBD. The primary motorsport venue for Western Australia, it hosts Supercars rounds and national series events, serving the isolated Perth metropolitan area that lies further from the eastern Australian motorsport centres than any other major Australian city. The warm, dry Perth climate and the circuit's flowing layout combine to produce a distinctive event character.
history: Wanneroo has hosted motorsport events since the early 1970s, when the original circuit opened on Crown land north of Perth. The circuit has been continuously developed and upgraded across five decades of use, with the Barbagallo naming reflecting a long-running corporate partnership. Supercars has used Wanneroo as its Western Australian round for many years. The Perth event's geographic isolation — Western Australia is closer to Singapore than to Sydney — gives it the character of a standalone event rather than part of a mainland circuit cluster.
notable_moments: Supercars Perth rounds providing championship-season action to Western Australia's motorsport community. The circuit's role as the only major permanent motorsport venue serving the most geographically isolated major city in the world. Various Australian national championship rounds contributing to West Australian driver development.

name: Phillip Island Grand Prix Circuit
bio: A windswept peninsula off the Victorian coast, Phillip Island's fast layout and compressed lap times make for relentless wheel-to-wheel racing in MotoGP and WSBK.
overview: Phillip Island Grand Prix Circuit is a 4.448 km permanent circuit on Phillip Island off the coast of Victoria, built on a headland above Bass Strait. One of the world's most spectacular motorcycle racing circuits, it is defined by fast, sweeping corners taken at enormous speed with the Southern Ocean visible beyond the barriers. The Gardner Straight leads into the double-apex turn at MG Corner; Lukey Heights is a crest taken blind at full throttle; the final chicane into the long Honda Hairpin finishes each lap. Wind off the Bass Strait is constant and unpredictable, making it one of the most conditions-sensitive circuits in any series.
history: Phillip Island hosted its first motorcycle Grand Prix in 1989. The circuit is built on a headland of an island best known for its little penguin colony — the contrast between wildlife tourism and motorcycle racing is unique in world motorsport. MotoGP and WorldSBK have both used Phillip Island as regular rounds, with the Australian MotoGP historically a late-season fixture. The coastal location means weather is genuinely variable — cold fronts from Antarctica can reach the island even in October, turning a sunny morning session into a wet, cold race afternoon.
notable_moments: Wayne Gardner's dominant home MotoGP victories in the late 1980s. Valentino Rossi's Phillip Island wins and his famous battle with Casey Stoner. Casey Stoner's circuit mastery — the Australian won here multiple times in front of his home crowd. The WorldSBK Australian rounds producing high-speed slipstream battles down the Gardner Straight. The Southern Ocean backdrop that makes aerial photography of Phillip Island racing among the most recognisable in motorcycle motorsport.

name: Phoenix Raceway
bio: Phoenix Raceway's one-mile D-oval in the Arizona desert is one of NASCAR's most technically demanding ovals. Its unique quad-oval shape and variable banking make every lane a different strategic calculation.
overview: Phoenix Raceway is a 1.0-mile oval in Avondale, Arizona, near Phoenix, with an unusual D-shaped layout featuring a unique dogleg in the back straight. Phoenix has hosted the NASCAR Cup championship finale since 2020 — the penultimate race in the four-driver elimination format that decides the champion. The desert climate produces consistent, dry racing conditions, and the cooler November temperatures of Phoenix's Championship Race weekend are a stark contrast to the heat of the Arizona summer.
history: Phoenix opened in 1964 and hosted NASCAR Cup races from 1988. IndyCar has also used the oval for championship events. The circuit's layout — including the distinctive kink on the back straight — creates a racing character unlike any other oval, where the asymmetry of the track challenges engineers' symmetric setup approach. Phoenix became the NASCAR championship finale under the current format, cementing its status as the most important single NASCAR track in terms of title outcomes.
notable_moments: NASCAR championship finales at Phoenix from 2020 onwards, producing intense four-driver battles for the title. Kyle Busch's multiple Phoenix victories. IndyCar oval events that produced dramatic results. The championship trophy presentations that make Phoenix the destination for NASCAR's season conclusion.

name: Pocono Raceway
bio: Pocono Raceway's triangular layout in the Pennsylvania mountains is unique in NASCAR — each of its three turns has different banking and a different radius, requiring three distinct setups in one lap.
overview: Pocono Raceway is a 2.5-mile triangular oval in Long Pond, Pennsylvania, known as The Tricky Triangle. Its three distinct corners — the Tunnel Turn, the Pocono Turn, and the Southern Turn — each have different banking angles, meaning cars require radically different suspension setups to handle all three effectively. No other oval in NASCAR produces the same asymmetric mechanical challenge. The long straightaways between corners produce very high speeds.
history: Pocono opened in 1971 and hosted its first NASCAR Cup race in 1974. The track's unusual triangular shape came from the available land and the creators' vision of a high-speed facility. The Pocono 400 and Pennsylvania 400 have been NASCAR fixtures for over fifty years. The circuit's Pennsylvania location, in the Pocono Mountains tourist region, has historically made it a popular destination race, with families combining a NASCAR weekend with broader mountain tourism.
notable_moments: Bill Elliott's near-record speeds at Pocono in the 1980s. The multiple NASCAR Cup crashes that the circuit's blind entries and asymmetric banking have produced over the years. Ryan Newman's significant crash at Pocono in 2009. The circuit's enduring presence on the NASCAR calendar despite its unusual format.

name: Autodromo Internacional do Algarve
bio: The Algarve circuit's sweeping blind crests and dramatic elevation changes make it one of the most visually spectacular venues on any calendar — and one of the hardest to learn.
overview: The Autodromo Internacional do Algarve is a 4.653 km circuit carved into the hillside above the Algarve coast of southern Portugal. Built in 2008, it is one of the most dramatic circuits in Europe — extreme elevation changes, blind crests, and banking make it immediately distinctive. The first corner is approached over a blind crest where cars effectively disappear from view. The circuit then cascades through a series of downhill sweeps and camber changes before climbing back to the start-finish straight. In wet conditions, it is genuinely treacherous.
history: Portimão was built as a private commercial venture to attract international motorsport to the Algarve. It initially hosted only national events and test sessions. Its profile changed when Formula 1 visited for the 2020 Portuguese Grand Prix — the first F1 race in Portugal since 1996. MotoGP added a Portuguese GP round in 2021. WEC and ELMS have used the circuit as a European venue. The circuit's dramatic geography has made it a favourite with drivers and television broadcasters, despite its relative newness on the international calendar.
notable_moments: The 2020 Portuguese Grand Prix that produced complex strategy results and Lewis Hamilton breaking Michael Schumacher's all-time wins record. The inaugural MotoGP Portuguese GP in 2021, which produced a tactical masterclass in tyre management. The circuit's Turn 1 blind crest — one of the most dramatic moments on any European circuit as cars appear to launch over the horizon.

name: Portland International Raceway
bio: Portland International Raceway has operated since 1961 on the Columbia River flood plain in northwest Oregon. Its distinctive carousel hairpin and flowing back section make it a unique circuit in the IndyCar calendar.
overview: Portland International Raceway is a 3.211 km permanent road course in Portland, Oregon, built on the site of Vanport, a city destroyed in a 1948 flood. The flat circuit in the Columbia River lowlands is compact, technical, and produces close IndyCar racing — the tight layout limits passing opportunities, making strategy and start position crucial. Portland hosts the Grand Prix of Portland as an IndyCar fixture and IMSA events.
history: Portland opened in 1960 on reclaimed flood land north of the city. It hosted CART IndyCar races from 1984 to 2003, becoming a Northwest motorsport institution. After nearly 15 years away, IndyCar returned to Portland in 2018 — the result of a community investment campaign to revive the race. IMSA also uses the circuit for road racing events. The circuit's flat, urban character is unlike any other IndyCar venue.
notable_moments: Al Unser Jr.'s victories at Portland during his CART championship years. The 2018 IndyCar return to Portland after 15 years. Controversial finish incidents at Portland involving Simon Pagenaud and Will Power. The circuit's community-led revival as a model for how American circuits can be restored to major championships.

name: Red Bull Ring
bio: Set in the Styrian Alps at 700 metres elevation, the circuit's short 4.3km lap and dramatic gradient changes produce some of the most compact and intense racing in Europe.
overview: The Red Bull Ring is a 4.318 km circuit built into the hillside of the Zeltweg valley in Styria, Austria. Deceptively brief — just nine corners — its extreme gradient changes and high-speed commitment zones make it one of the most demanding circuits per kilometre. The main straight climbs steeply from the pit exit to Turn 1, where cars arrive over the crest at over 300 km/h before braking hard for a tight right-hander. The spectator experience is exceptional: the natural bowl of the valley provides grandstand views across the entire circuit.
history: The site hosted the original Österreichring from 1969 to 1987 — a 5.9 km sweeping mountain layout considered one of the most beautiful circuits in F1 history. It was rebuilt as the shorter A1 Ring in the 1990s, hosting F1 from 1997 to 2003. Energy drink company Red Bull purchased the circuit in 2011 and rebuilt it to current specification with lengthened straights and revised corners, reopening in 2011. The circuit returned to the F1 calendar in 2014 and has hosted both the Austrian GP and, in some seasons, a second event (the Styrian GP). MotoGP and DTM have also used the circuit as a regular venue.
notable_moments: Niki Lauda's spiritual home — the Austrian champion raced here on the original Österreichring in front of his home crowd. The 2021 season where two races at the Red Bull Ring produced contact between Max Verstappen and Lewis Hamilton in consecutive events. Charles Leclerc's dominant Austrian GP win in 2022. The spectacular hillside scenery that makes this one of the most photographed circuits on any calendar.

name: Richmond Raceway
bio: Richmond Raceway's 0.75-mile D-oval is known for consistent late-race battles and tire degradation that rewards pit strategy. Its smooth surface and multiple grooves make it one of the most reliably entertaining ovals on the schedule.
overview: Richmond Raceway is a 0.75-mile D-shaped oval in Richmond, Virginia — America's Premier Short Track. The concrete and asphalt combined surface, moderate banking, and three-quarter-mile distance produce racing that blends elements of superspeedway and short track — fast enough for drafting to matter, tight enough for mechanical grip to dominate. Richmond's night race is one of NASCAR's most atmospheric events, with the oval's enclosed shape amplifying the crowd noise and the race's post-dark drama.
history: Richmond hosted its first NASCAR race in 1953. The oval changed shape and surface over the decades, settling into its current three-quarter-mile D-shape in the 1980s. The circuit is located within the Richmond International Raceway complex, embedded in the suburban landscape of Virginia's capital. NASCAR's fall Richmond race is traditionally a playoff cut-off point — one of the last chances for drivers to make the field before elimination rounds begin.
notable_moments: Richard Petty's domination of Richmond in the 1970s. Dale Earnhardt Sr.'s multiple victories here. The spring and fall Richmond races as consistent NASCAR playoff pressure points. The atmosphere of Richmond night races with the full oval grandstands surrounding the circuit.

name: Road America
bio: Wisconsin's 6.5km permanent road course is among the longest and fastest in North America, with wide sweeping corners and elevation changes that reward commitment.
overview: Road America is a 6.515 km permanent road course in the lake district of Wisconsin near Elkhart Lake — one of the longest and most respected circuits in North America. Carved through the Wisconsin woodland, the circuit uses natural elevation changes and wide, open corners to produce a driving experience unlike any other American road course. IndyCar's Elkhart Lake race and IMSA events use the full circuit. Drivers consistently rank Road America among their favourite circuits on any calendar.
history: Road America opened in 1955, created by Clif Tufte on farmland near Elkhart Lake after road racing on the county roads of the area became impractical. The circuit's character has changed remarkably little since opening — the same ribbon of asphalt through the same trees, with improvements to facilities and safety infrastructure. Road America hosted CART IndyCar for many years and has hosted the modern IndyCar series. The Vintage races at Road America, each August, bring thousands of historic racing cars to the circuit for the largest vintage motorsport event in North America.
notable_moments: Michael Andretti's multiple CART victories at Road America. The annual Vintage weekend attracting the largest collection of historic racing cars in North America. IndyCar races where the long circuit produces strategy complexity and fuel saving that generates unpredictable results.

name: Michelin Raceway Road Atlanta
bio: Georgia's demanding permanent circuit is defined by its downhill compression at the back straight esses. Home of the Petit Le Mans, Road Atlanta's combination of flat-out commitment and blind corners makes it a driver's track.
overview: Michelin Raceway Road Atlanta is a 4.088 km permanent road course north of Atlanta in Braselton, Georgia. One of America's most challenging and exciting road circuits, it is defined by blind crests, dramatic elevation changes, and corners that demand full commitment — particularly the 10A/10B Esses and the run through Turn 12. Road Atlanta hosts the Petit Le Mans — the IMSA season finale, a 10-hour endurance race — which is one of the premier endurance events in American motorsport.
history: Road Atlanta opened in 1970 and immediately established itself as one of America's most technically demanding circuits. It hosted Can-Am, Trans-Am, and IndyCar events in its early years before becoming the home of the Petit Le Mans (named in tribute to the French race) from 1998 onwards. The circuit changed ownership multiple times before its current operation. Michelin's naming rights partnership came in the modern era. The Petit Le Mans has produced some of IMSA's most dramatic finishes.
notable_moments: The inaugural Petit Le Mans in 1998. Multiple Petit Le Mans finishes decided in the final hour with championship implications. The circuit's fearsome reputation — Martin Donnelly suffered a life-threatening accident here during a Formula 3000 race in 1990.
