IndyCar races that produced dramatic results on Road Atlanta's unforgiving layout.

name: Sachsenring
bio: A tight, left-hand-dominated circuit in eastern Germany that became the circuit of Marc Márquez, who won the German Grand Prix ten consecutive times. Its short 3.7km lap produces intense, technical riding.
overview: The Sachsenring is a 3.671 km permanent circuit in the Erzgebirge hills of Saxony, Germany. It is the home of the German MotoGP — and Marc Marquez's personal circuit. Of the 13 corners, 10 turn left, rewarding riders who favour left-side tyre loading and a physically aggressive style. The shortest circuit on the MotoGP calendar, it is also one of the most demanding, with the constant left-hand bias loading the body asymmetrically across the entire race distance.
history: Motorcycle racing in the Sachsenring area dates back to the 1920s on a road circuit through Hohenstein-Ernstthal. The circuit became one of the most prominent venues in East German motorsport during the GDR era, hosting the East German motorcycle GP as part of the old world championship calendar. After German reunification, the circuit was modernised and a permanent facility built; the current layout opened in 1996. MotoGP has used Sachsenring since 1998. The circuit's history extends across nearly a century of German motorsport culture.
notable_moments: Marc Marquez's extraordinary record at this circuit — 11 consecutive premier class victories at one point, a dominance without parallel at any circuit in modern MotoGP. The circuit's ongoing role as one of the most partisan and atmospherically distinctive stops on the MotoGP calendar.

name: Streets of San Diego
bio: The Streets of San Diego circuit runs through downtown San Diego's Embarcadero and harbor district. One of IndyCar's newer street circuits, added to provide a West Coast presence on the calendar.
overview: Streets of San Diego is a temporary street circuit in San Diego, California, bringing IndyCar racing to America's eighth-largest city and the primary major California market outside the existing Long Beach event. The circuit runs through the downtown waterfront area, adjacent to the San Diego Bay, producing a setting with the Pacific backdrop and the city's mild climate that contrasts with the street circuit venues further inland.
history: Streets of San Diego joined the IndyCar calendar as part of the series' California market expansion strategy, aiming to establish IndyCar presence in major Pacific Coast markets. San Diego's position as a large, affluent coastal city with no previous IndyCar history made it an attractive commercial opportunity. The waterfront circuit location links the race to the city's maritime identity and its established tourism infrastructure.
notable_moments: IndyCar's arrival in San Diego as a new market for American open-wheel racing. The circuit's waterfront character in America's most consistently pleasant-weather major city.

name: Sandown Raceway
bio: Sandown Raceway in suburban Melbourne has hosted Supercars since the 1960s. Its undulating layout and Sandown 500 endurance race are fixtures in the Australian touring car calendar.
overview: Sandown Raceway is a 3.100 km permanent circuit in Melbourne's southeastern suburbs, at Springvale South in Victoria. One of Australia's oldest active motorsport venues, it has hosted Supercars racing for over six decades and serves as the traditional warm-up event for the Bathurst 1000 — the Sandown 500 endurance race prepares teams and the co-driver pairings that will race at Mount Panorama the following month.
history: Sandown opened in 1962 on the site of a former horse racing track, making use of existing grandstand infrastructure and the large open infield. The circuit hosted Australian Grand Prix races in the 1960s and has been a continuous presence on the Australian motorsport calendar since. The Sandown 500 was historically known as the Phillip Morris International (later Sandown 500) and has served as the traditional endurance season opener for Supercars. The circuit's heritage and its proximity to Melbourne give it a loyal Victorian fanbase.
notable_moments: The Sandown 500 as the traditional precursor to Bathurst — co-driver pairings testing their combinations before the Great Race. The circuit's 1960s history when it hosted Australian GP support events. Multiple Supercars title contenders using Sandown as a pivotal round in close championship seasons.

name: Sanya Street Circuit
bio: Sanya's waterfront Formula E circuit on Hainan Island was part of the series' push into China's booming motorsport market.
overview: Sanya Street Circuit was a 2.888 km temporary street circuit in Sanya, the resort city at the southern tip of Hainan island, China. Used for Formula E's Sanya ePrix from 2018 to 2019, it ran along the Haitang Bay waterfront — a tropical beach resort setting that gave it a visual character unlike any other Formula E venue. The circuit ran through the hotel and shopping district of Haitang Bay, with the South China Sea visible beyond the circuit barriers.
history: Formula E's Sanya ePrix was part of the championship's early-season calendar during the 2017-18 and 2018-19 seasons, capitalising on China's enthusiasm for electric vehicle technology and the commercial appeal of the Hainan Free Trade Zone. Sanya was also part of China's broader ambition to position Hainan as an international luxury tourism and duty-free shopping destination — hosting a Formula E race fitted the international profile the government sought. The ePrix did not continue beyond 2019.
notable_moments: Formula E racing on the Hainan tropical waterfront — one of the most unusual settings in the championship's history. Jean-Éric Vergne's 2019 victory. The circuit's brief but visually distinctive run as China's southernmost motor racing venue.

name: Sebring International Raceway
bio: Florida's wartime airfield turned race circuit has hosted endurance racing since 1950. The concrete joins between runway sections make the 12 Hours of Sebring the most punishing ride quality of any endurance race in North America.
overview: Sebring International Raceway is a 6.019 km permanent circuit on the site of Hendricks Army Airfield in central Florida, home of the 12 Hours of Sebring — one of the great endurance races and one of the oldest in the United States. The circuit uses the original WWII airfield concrete, producing a surface of legendary bumpiness and surface irregularity that destroys cars, tyres, and drivers with equal indifference. Sebring in March is hot, dusty, and relentless — an endurance test in the most literal sense.
history: Sebring has hosted endurance races since 1950 when Alec Ulmann organised a 6-hour race on the airfield using the original military runway markings. The 12 Hours format began in 1952 and has run continuously since. The circuit incorporates the original military runways, taxiways, and perimeter roads — the joins and repairs over decades have created the notorious Sebring surface that breaks components no other circuit tests. Sebring, Le Mans, and Daytona form the traditional Triple Crown of endurance racing.
notable_moments: Juan Manuel Fangio's 1956 victory. The 1966 race between Ford and Ferrari. Peter Gregg's domination in the IMSA era. The annual tradition of the Sebring 12 Hours as the traditional season opener for IMSA. The circuit's surface condition — genuinely one of the most demanding in world motorsport — breaking cars that would survive anywhere else without incident.

name: Sepang International Circuit
bio: Hermann Tilke's 1999 tropical masterpiece hosted the Malaysian Grand Prix for 19 years. Thunderstorm afternoon races, extreme humidity, and distinctive twin grandstand became defining images of early 2000s F1.
overview: Sepang International Circuit is a 5.543 km permanent circuit 60km south of Kuala Lumpur, adjacent to Kuala Lumpur International Airport. One of Hermann Tilke's most celebrated designs — built in 1999 — it features sweeping high-speed corners, a very long main straight, dramatic covered grandstands, and a tropical climate that creates intense tyre and brake conditions. Sepang hosted F1's Malaysian Grand Prix from 1999 to 2017 and continues as one of Asia's premier venues for MotoGP, WorldSBK, and WEC.
history: Sepang was built to host the 1999 Malaysian Grand Prix — the circuit was designed and built from scratch in eleven months, an extraordinary construction timeline. Hermann Tilke's design introduced features that became his signature: wide main straight, multiple overtaking possibilities, and technically demanding sector changes. Sepang immediately became regarded as one of the best of Tilke's circuits. F1 held the Malaysian GP here from 1999 to 2017, when Malaysia withdrew from the F1 calendar citing commercial factors. MotoGP, WorldSBK, and WEC remain.
notable_moments: Mika Häkkinen and Michael Schumacher's 1999 battle at the inaugural Malaysian GP. Marco Simoncelli's fatal accident during the 2011 MotoGP Malaysian GP — one of the most shocking moments in modern motorcycle racing. The annual MotoGP Malaysian GP serving as a near-final-round title decider in multiple championship seasons. Marc Marquez's multiple victories at Sepang.

name: Shanghai International Circuit
bio: The Shanghai International Circuit opened in 2004 with a distinctive sweep-shaped main grandstand. Its long back straight and tight final hairpin produce consistent overtaking. Formula E uses a shorter inner layout of the same facility.
overview: Shanghai International Circuit is a 5.451 km circuit in the Jiading District of Shanghai, designed by Hermann Tilke and opened in 2004. Viewed from above, the circuit's main straight and Turn 1 form the shape of a snail shell — an instantly recognisable design concept. The 1.2 km back straight and Turn 6 hairpin create one of F1's best overtaking opportunities. China's largest city provides enormous commercial potential for motorsport, and the circuit has hosted WEC alongside F1.
history: Shanghai was built for the inaugural Chinese Grand Prix in 2004, part of F1's expansion into the world's most populous country. The circuit was constructed on reclaimed marshland in Jiading, with thousands of piles driven into the ground to stabilise the foundation — a construction challenge that drove up costs significantly. The circuit has hosted the Chinese GP every year it has been held, though COVID disruptions affected the calendar significantly in 2020-2022. WEC's 6 Hours of Shanghai forms part of the championship's Asian leg alongside Fuji and Bahrain.
notable_moments: Michael Schumacher's multiple victories at Shanghai driving for Ferrari. Nico Rosberg's dominant 2016 win en route to his championship. The 2024 Chinese Grand Prix — the first since 2019 due to COVID — producing a sprint format weekend and a dramatic Max Verstappen victory. Lewis Hamilton's wins at Shanghai during his championship years. WEC endurance battles in the coastal Chinese climate.

name: Silverstone Circuit
bio: A wartime airfield turned into the home of British motorsport. It hosted the very first FIA World Championship round in May 1950 and has barely missed a year since.
overview: Silverstone is the home of British motorsport, a 5.891 km circuit built on the site of RAF Silverstone — a wartime Wellington bomber training base. It hosted the very first FIA Formula One World Championship race in May 1950, attended by King George VI, and has remained a permanent fixture on the F1 calendar since. Today it is one of the fastest circuits in the world, defined by the demanding Maggotts-Becketts-Chapel sequence, two long straights, and weather that can change in minutes above the open Northamptonshire plain.
history: The circuit began life informally in 1947, when a group of local enthusiasts organised an impromptu meeting on the abandoned runways — the "Mutton Grand Prix," named after a sheep that wandered onto the track. The Royal Automobile Club formally established a circuit from the runways in 1948, and the first British Grand Prix followed that October. The British Racing Drivers' Club took over operation from 1952. The classic layout was established by the 1950s, with significant rebuilds following in 1975 (Woodcote chicane after the 1973 pile-up), 1990 (the sweeping new Maggotts-Becketts-Chapel complex), and 1994 (safety revisions post-Imola). The current layout centred on the Wing pit complex debuted in 2010, funded by a long-term F1 contract.
notable_moments: The 1950 British Grand Prix — the first FIA Formula One World Championship race in history, won by Giuseppe Farina for Alfa Romeo. The 1973 British GP pile-up triggered by Jody Scheckter at Woodcote. Keke Rosberg's 160 mph average qualifying lap in 1985. Nigel Mansell's wheel-to-wheel battle with Ayrton Senna in 1987. Lewis Hamilton's multiple home victories. The 2022 British GP opening lap crash involving Zhou Guanyu's terrifying barrel-roll, followed by a dramatic red-flagged restart.

name: Marina Bay Street Circuit
bio: Formula 1's first permanent night race since 1985, Marina Bay's floodlit street circuit is one of the most physically demanding on the calendar. Ninety-two minutes of humidity, walls, and zero margin for error.
overview: Marina Bay Street Circuit is a 5.063 km street circuit through the Marina Bay waterfront district of Singapore. It hosts Formula 1's only night race — under 1,500 lighting rigs that illuminate the circuit against the backdrop of the Singapore skyline, the Supertrees of Gardens by the Bay, and the Fullerton Hotel. One of the most visually spectacular settings in F1, it is also one of the most physically demanding events of the season — slow, bumpy, and hot even at night, with 23 corners across a lap that averages nearly two minutes.
history: The Singapore Grand Prix debuted in 2008 as Formula 1's first night race, a concept that required two years of preparation and the installation of permanent lighting infrastructure around the circuit route. The debut race was immediately controversialised by Renault's orchestration of a deliberate crash by Nelson Piquet Jr. to trigger a safety car that benefited Fernando Alonso — the Crashgate scandal. Singapore has hosted F1 every year since 2008 except 2020 and 2021 (COVID). The Marina Bay circuit winds through the financial district, past Raffles Place and the Esplanade arts complex, through an underpass beneath the grandstands, and along the waterfront.
notable_moments: The 2008 Crashgate scandal — Nelson Piquet Jr.'s deliberate crash orchestrated by Renault to favour Alonso. Sebastian Vettel's multiple victories here. The 2017 race where Vettel, Verstappen, and Räikkönen all collided at Turn 1 on the opening lap. The circuit's unique atmosphere — hot night air, city lights reflected in the marina, the roar of F1 cars against the Singapore skyline.

name: Snetterton Circuit
bio: Norfolk's Snetterton circuit was substantially redesigned in 2011. Its long back straight and technical final sector provide a varied challenge that rewards both outright pace and mechanical balance.
overview: Snetterton Circuit is a 3.623 km permanent circuit (300 configuration) in the Norfolk Breckland, built on a former WWII airfield. It hosts BTCC and British Superbike Championship rounds as one of the longer circuits on the British national calendar. The flat Norfolk countryside and prevailing easterly winds off the North Sea make Snetterton one of the more exposed and weather-affected venues in Britain — conditions that can change the racing character significantly.
history: Snetterton was converted from RAF Snetterton Heath, a WWII bomber airfield, for motorsport use in the 1950s. The circuit expanded and developed over the following decades, with the current 300 layout using the full perimeter of the site. Motorsport Vision invested in the circuit in the 2000s, adding facilities and upgrading the track surface. The Norfolk location — away from the major population centres — gives Snetterton a cult following among dedicated British motorsport fans.
notable_moments: BTCC rounds at Snetterton contributing to season-long title battles. The circuit's airfield heritage visible in its long, flat straight sections. BSB battles at the longer circuit configuration where power and aerodynamics play a greater role than at tighter British venues.

name: Sonoma Raceway
bio: Sonoma Raceway's main 2.52-mile road course through the California wine country is one of North America's most loved permanent circuits. Its carousel, elevation changes, and setting make it a driver and fan favourite. NASCAR runs a shorter layout bypassing the Carousel.
overview: Sonoma Raceway is a 3.840 km permanent road course in the wine country hills north of San Francisco, California. One of two road course events on the NASCAR Cup schedule (alongside Watkins Glen), Sonoma brings an entirely different set of skills to the Cup field — braking, downforce, and left-right coordination rather than oval momentum management. The circuit weaves through the Sonoma hills with a combination of tight hairpins and flowing uphill sections.
history: Sonoma opened in 1968 as Sears Point Raceway and hosted its first NASCAR Cup event in 1989. The circuit has been a consistent annual fixture for NASCAR, with the Toyota Save Mart 350 (and its various naming iterations) bringing oval specialists to California wine country every summer. IMSA also uses the circuit. The track changed its name to Infineon Raceway under Compuware ownership before the current Sonoma Raceway identity. IndyCar previously held events here before removing the circuit from its calendar.
notable_moments: Jeff Gordon's six Sonoma victories — the record at the circuit. Tony Stewart's road course mastery at Sonoma. Chase Elliott's multiple wins demonstrating the new generation's road course ability. The circuit's role as the annual test of whether oval specialists can adapt to road courses.

name: Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps
bio: Seven kilometres of Ardennes forest, Eau Rouge, and a microclimate capable of producing four seasons in a single lap. The benchmark circuit in European motorsport.
overview: Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps consistently tops polls as the greatest racing circuit in the world, a reputation built on its combination of unforgiving elevation changes, high-speed corners, and notoriously unpredictable weather. Carved into the Ardennes forest of Wallonia at 7.004 km, it is the longest circuit on the Formula 1 calendar and the anchor event of the WEC European season. No other circuit offers the same range of challenges in a single lap: flat-out high-speed sweepers, heavy braking zones, blind crests, and weather that can vary dramatically between corners.
history: The original Spa circuit was a 14.9 km triangular road course using public roads between the towns of Spa, Stavelot and Francorchamps, opened in 1921. It hosted its first Belgian Grand Prix in 1925 and was considered one of the most dangerous circuits in the world for the next five decades. A revised, shorter permanent circuit of 6.949 km opened in 1979, built entirely within the Stavelot commune and incorporating the legendary Eau Rouge/Raidillon complex into a new layout. The circuit underwent further safety revisions through the 1990s and 2000s, with the bus stop chicane reconfigured multiple times. A major redesign in 2022 saw the La Source hairpin widened, the Raidillon approach rebuilt with extended run-off areas, and the final chicane reconstructed. The rebuilt Raidillon — which had witnessed fatal accidents in 2021 — was a turning point in how modern circuits balance spectacle with safety.
notable_moments: Ayrton Senna and Alain Prost's race-ending collision on the opening lap of the 1990 Belgian Grand Prix. Michael Schumacher's F1 debut in 1991, replacing an injured Jordan driver. The rain-soaked 2021 Belgian Grand Prix, run behind the safety car for two laps due to persistent conditions and controversially declared a half-points result. The 2022 WEC round that produced a multi-class battle decided in the final hour.

name: St. Petersburg Street Circuit
bio: Downtown St. Petersburg's temporary circuit uses part of the Albert Whitted Airport runway and waterfront streets. One of the most visually distinctive street venues in IndyCar, with palm trees and the bay as backdrop.
overview: The St. Petersburg Street Circuit is a 2.953 km temporary street circuit in downtown St. Petersburg, Florida, using public roads through the waterfront district alongside Tampa Bay. The Firestone Grand Prix of St. Petersburg traditionally opens the IndyCar season — making St. Pete the circuit where the year's competitive order is first revealed and where new driver-team combinations face their first real test. The downtown setting, warm February weather, and season-opener significance make St. Pete one of IndyCar's most important annual events.
history: St. Petersburg has hosted IndyCar since 2003, becoming the established season opener for the series. The circuit's waterfront location, with the Tampa Bay waterfront and the downtown skyline as backdrop, gives it a visual quality that matches its schedule importance. Formula E also used a St. Petersburg circuit for several seasons. The race has produced a long string of dramatic season-opening battles that set championship narratives for the year.
notable_moments: Multiple IndyCar championship-season-defining opens at St. Pete. Scott Dixon's multiple victories reinforcing his dominance at season openers. The circuit's role as the bellwether for which teams and drivers arrive at the start of each season in strong form. First-race drama that has launched and derailed championship campaigns across multiple eras.

name: Sportsland SUGO
bio: Sportsland Sugo in Miyagi prefecture is a compact, technical Japanese road circuit set in the hills north of Sendai. Its tight layout and limited run-off demand precision and have made it a staple of domestic Japanese racing.
overview: Sportsland SUGO is a 3.704 km permanent circuit in the Sendai hills of Miyagi Prefecture in the Tohoku region of northern Japan. Technical, hilly, and challenging, it is one of the most demanding circuits on the Japanese domestic calendar — tight, twisting, and built into the natural contours of the hillside without compromise. Super GT rounds here consistently produce unpredictable results, as the circuit's character strips away aerodynamic advantages and rewards mechanical grip and driver precision.
history: Sportsland SUGO opened in 1973 and has hosted Japanese domestic motorsport continuously since. The circuit is located in the Sendai metropolitan area, serving the motorsport community of northeastern Japan — a region more commonly associated with the aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami that devastated the coastline 60km east of the circuit. The SUGO round of Super GT is considered one of the most technically demanding of the season by drivers, valued for its differentiation from the faster, more aero-dependent circuits like Fuji and Suzuka.
notable_moments: Super GT battles at SUGO producing season-affecting results through the circuit's tendency to equalise machinery. The circuit's longevity as a Tohoku regional motorsport institution. The contrast between SUGO's tight hillside character and Japan's other major circuits.

name: Suzuka Circuit
bio: John Hugenholtz's figure-of-eight layout from 1962 is a driver's circuit: the Esses, 130R, and a punishing final chicane reward precision over raw power.
overview: Suzuka Circuit is a 5.807 km figure-eight circuit in Mie Prefecture, Japan — the only figure-eight circuit used in Formula 1. Owned by Honda, it is considered by drivers, engineers, and fans to be among the greatest circuits in the world: technically perfect, physically demanding, and historically loaded with the weight of F1 championship deciders. The Esses, Degner Curve, the 130R, the Spoon Curve, and the Casio Triangle are a collection of corners referenced as a benchmark of circuit design quality.
history: Suzuka was built by Honda in 1962 as a test track for the company's road cars and motorcycles. It was designed by John Hugenholtz, a Dutch circuit architect, and its figure-eight layout — where the track crosses itself on an overpass — was an innovative solution to maximising circuit length within a constrained footprint. F1 first visited in 1987 and Suzuka has been the home of the Japanese Grand Prix almost without interruption since. The circuit has hosted more championship-deciding moments than any other single venue in F1 history.
notable_moments: The 1988, 1989, and 1990 collisions between Ayrton Senna and Alain Prost that defined and ended their rivalry. Schumacher's multiple championships clinched here. The 1994 season finale where Damon Hill and Schumacher's championship went to the final lap. The 2005 and 2006 Japanese GPs in the Alonso-Schumacher era. Lewis Hamilton's 2015 championship clinch here. The WEC 6 Hours of Suzuka as a key endurance calendar round.

name: Sydney Motorsport Park
bio: Sydney Motorsport Park at Eastern Creek is the primary permanent circuit in New South Wales. Its multiple configurations and large pit complex have made it the centre of motorsport infrastructure on Australia's east coast.
overview: Sydney Motorsport Park is a 3.931 km permanent circuit in Eastern Creek, 40km west of Sydney's CBD. One of Australia's most multi-use motorsport facilities, it hosts Supercars, WorldSBK, and numerous national series events. The circuit's proximity to Sydney's western suburbs provides access to Australia's largest population centre, though the industrial character of the Eastern Creek precinct is a contrast to the scenic settings of Mount Panorama or Phillip Island.
history: The circuit opened in 1991 as Eastern Creek Raceway, designed for the New South Wales government as a replacement for the aging Amaroo Park circuit. It was built to international standards capable of hosting grand prix motorcycle and touring car events. Renamed Sydney Motorsport Park under a government rebrand, it has consistently hosted Supercars rounds (historically the NRMA Sydney 500 was a finale event) and WorldSBK Australian rounds at various points. The circuit continues as New South Wales' primary motorsport venue.
notable_moments: Multiple Supercars season finales held at Sydney Motorsport Park. WorldSBK Australian rounds bringing motorcycle championship action to the western Sydney venue. The circuit's role as the primary motorsport development venue for New South Wales drivers and teams.

name: Talladega Superspeedway
bio: At 2.66 miles, Talladega is the longest oval in NASCAR. Steep banking and restrictor plates compress 40 cars into a single hurtling pack — the result is the most chaotic, unpredictable racing in the sport.
overview: Talladega Superspeedway is a 2.66-mile superspeedway in Talladega, Alabama — the largest NASCAR circuit by size and one of the fastest in the world. The 33° banked turns allow cars to reach speeds approaching 200 mph while running in tight drafting packs. The restrictor plate/superspeedway format means every car is artificially equalised in speed, creating multi-car packs where the slipstream effect produces extreme close-quarters racing and the inevitable "Big One" — a multi-car pile-up that can take out half the field in seconds.
history: Talladega opened in 1969, immediately the biggest and fastest track in NASCAR. The inaugural race was boycotted by the Professional Drivers Association over safety concerns, but Richard Brickhouse won in a car owned by Ray Fox. The track's extreme speed led to the introduction of restrictor plates in 1988 following Bill Elliott's 212 mph qualifying run — the fastest in NASCAR history. Restrictor plate racing fundamentally changed the nature of Talladega events; today the superspeedway format creates a unique product unlike any other NASCAR race.
notable_moments: Bill Elliott's 212.809 mph qualifying lap in 1987. Dale Earnhardt Sr.'s extraordinary ability to find the draft and position at Talladega — he won here ten times. The 2000 fall race's massive multi-car crash. The 2009 fall race "Big One" that ended with Brad Keselowski's victory. Bubba Wallace's 2021 win — his first Cup victory. The perpetual chaos of every Talladega weekend.

name: Symmons Plains Raceway
bio: Symmons Plains Raceway is a compact 2.4km circuit in northern Tasmania. Despite its small size, its high-speed bowl section and flowing infield make it one of the most crowd-pleasing circuits on the Supercars calendar.
overview: Symmons Plains Raceway is a 2.400 km permanent circuit near Launceston in northern Tasmania, Australia. The shortest circuit on the Supercars calendar, it produces some of the most compressed and intense racing of the season — the limited track length means lapping backmarkers is a constant challenge, and strategy calls have outsized effect when pit stop time is proportionally larger relative to lap time. The Tasmanian countryside setting and the Apple Isle's motorsport tradition make the Tasmania round one of the more distinctive stops on the Supercars calendar.
history: Symmons Plains has hosted Supercars racing for decades, making it one of the longest-running venues on the Australian touring car calendar. The circuit's location in northern Tasmania, near Launceston, means competitors and fans must fly or take the ferry from mainland Australia — giving the Tasmania round a distinctive logistics character compared to mainland events. Tasmania's motorsport culture is rooted in the island's geographic isolation, which historically produced its own racing championship structure independent of mainland series.
notable_moments: Supercars Tasmania rounds producing sprint-format racing where every tactical decision is amplified by the short circuit. The circuit's compactness generating overtaking opportunities and close racing across every round. The unique atmosphere of Australian Supercars racing in the Apple Isle.

name: Bruce McLaren Motorsport Park
bio: Bruce McLaren Motorsport Park in Taupō, New Zealand, is the country's primary permanent road circuit. Set beside Lake Taupō, it honours the hometown of the greatest racing driver New Zealand has ever produced.
overview: Bruce McLaren Motorsport Park is a 3.198 km permanent circuit on the shores of Lake Taupo in the centre of New Zealand's North Island. Named after Bruce McLaren — the New Zealand racing driver and constructor who founded McLaren and died testing at Goodwood in 1970 — the circuit sits in one of New Zealand's most dramatic landscapes, with the volcanic plateau and lake as its backdrop. It is the primary venue for New Zealand national motorsport championships and hosts occasional international events.
history: The circuit near Taupo has hosted motorsport for decades, progressively upgraded into a permanent facility of international standard. It was renamed Bruce McLaren Motorsport Park in tribute to New Zealand's greatest motorsport export. The circuit's geographic position — central North Island, away from the major population centres of Auckland, Wellington, and Christchurch — limits its audience draw, but the natural setting and the Bruce McLaren heritage association give it a significance beyond its size. New Zealand has produced a remarkable number of great racing drivers relative to its population — McLaren, Denny Hulme, Chris Amon, Scott Dixon — and Taupo sits within that tradition.
notable_moments: The circuit's role as New Zealand's primary permanent motorsport venue for national championship development. The naming tribute to Bruce McLaren, reflecting New Zealand's pride in a son who founded one of F1's most storied teams. Events that bring international competitors to the North Island volcanic plateau.

name: The Bend Motorsport Park
bio: The Bend Motorsport Park in South Australia opened in 2018 as the country's newest and most comprehensively equipped permanent circuit. At 7.77km in its full configuration, it is the longest circuit in Australia.
overview: The Bend Motorsport Park is a 4.950 km permanent circuit at Tailem Bend in South Australia, 70km southeast of Adelaide. Opened in 2018, it is Australia's longest permanent race circuit and its newest major motorsport facility. The circuit features multiple layouts — the full race circuit, a shorter east and west configuration, and a rallycross track — making it one of the most versatile motorsport venues in the southern hemisphere. Its modern facilities, wide pit lane, and extensive run-off areas reflect contemporary circuit design philosophy.
history: The Bend was developed by Sam Shahin, a South Australian businessman, as a commercial motorsport complex designed to attract international and national events. Built on former agricultural land near Tailem Bend, it opened in 2018 with an inaugural Supercars round. The project represented a significant private investment in South Australian motorsport infrastructure, complementing the existing Adelaide street circuit event. The circuit quickly established itself as a Supercars venue and has hosted GT World Challenge Australia rounds.
notable_moments: The inaugural 2018 Supercars The Bend 500 — the first major event at Australia's newest major circuit. GT World Challenge Australia rounds on the full 4.950 km layout. The circuit's development as a multi-discipline venue, with the rallycross facility adding a dimension not available at other Australian permanent circuits.

name: Thruxton Circuit
bio: Hampshire's wartime airfield circuit is the fastest track on the BTCC calendar. Its open, flowing layout and long Allard complex generate the highest average speeds in British touring car racing.
overview: Thruxton Circuit is a 3.812 km permanent circuit in Hampshire, England — the fastest circuit on the BTCC calendar by average speed. Built on the former RAF Thruxton airfield, its wide, flowing layout with very few slow corners produces average speeds that stress car and tyre to the maximum. The high-speed Allard and Campbell-Croft complex at the back of the circuit is one of the most demanding sustained sections in British club racing.
history: Thruxton was converted from RAF Thruxton for motorsport use in the 1960s, with its first car race held in 1968. The circuit's airfield origins give it the wide runoffs and flowing layout typical of wartime airfield conversions. BTCC has used Thruxton as a regular venue for much of the championship's history, and British Superbike also uses the circuit. The Hampshire countryside setting — between Andover and Amesbury — is pleasant but exposed, with wind a constant factor affecting race outcomes.
notable_moments: BTCC rounds at Thruxton producing the highest-speed touring car racing on the British calendar. Title battles where Thruxton's high-speed demands separate genuine front-runners from the midfield. The visual spectacle of BTCC and BSB machines at near-maximum speed through the Campbell-Croft complex.

name: Tokyo E-Prix Circuit
bio: Formula E's Tokyo E-Prix street circuit runs through the city's coastal district, threading between exhibition halls and waterfront infrastructure. It represents the series' ambition to race in the heart of the world's largest city.
overview: The Tokyo E-Prix Circuit is a temporary street circuit in the Minato Ward of Tokyo, Japan, used for the Formula E Tokyo ePrix from 2024. The circuit runs through the waterfront area of Shibaura and Daiba, incorporating sections of the Rinkai Road alongside Tokyo Bay. Formula E's arrival in Tokyo — the world's most populous metropolitan area — was one of the championship's most significant venue additions, bringing electric racing to a city that is both a global capital and the headquarters of major manufacturers including Toyota, Honda, and Nissan.
history: Formula E's Tokyo ePrix was announced as a landmark addition to the championship's calendar, reflecting both Japan's importance to the global automotive industry and the Japanese government's commitment to electric vehicle adoption. The circuit is a temporary construction on public roads around the Tokyo waterfront, deconstructed after race weekend. The race debuted in 2024 and established Tokyo as a key anchor of the Formula E Asian calendar alongside Jakarta and Seoul.
notable_moments: Formula E's inaugural Tokyo ePrix in 2024 — the first world championship single-seater race in the Japanese capital. The circuit's waterfront setting with Tokyo Bay providing a spectacular backdrop. The arrival of Formula E in the backyard of Honda, Toyota, and Nissan — all with ties to the championship.

name: Reid Park Street Circuit
bio: Reid Park Street Circuit in North Queensland has hosted Supercars since 2009. Its tight barriers and limited overtaking zones make strategy and the safety car decisive factors in the Townsville 500.
overview: Reid Park Street Circuit is a temporary street circuit in Reid Park, central Townsville, in tropical north Queensland. The NTI Townsville 500 is one of the most popular rounds on the Supercars calendar — the compact circuit, the north Queensland heat, and the passionate local crowd create a distinctive event atmosphere. The circuit is tight and demanding, with barriers close on multiple sections and limited room for error, producing the aggressive contact and tyre-grinding racing typical of Supercars street events.
history: Townsville has hosted Supercars events using the Reid Park street circuit for many years, establishing north Queensland as a regular stop on the national touring car calendar. The event reflects Supercars' commitment to taking racing to regional Queensland cities beyond Brisbane and the Gold Coast, building motorsport audiences in tropical communities where the summer racing series is a major annual event. Reid Park — normally a public green space — is transformed into a race circuit each year with temporary infrastructure.
notable_moments: Supercars Townsville 500 races producing dramatic street circuit battles in the north Queensland heat. The event's reputation as one of the most colourful stops on the Supercars calendar — tropical location, committed local crowd, and the aggressive street circuit format combining to create a distinctive race weekend character.

name: Circuit Ricardo Tormo
bio: Circuit Ricardo Tormo near Valencia is one of MotoGP's slowest circuits, its tight infield requiring maximum braking and acceleration. It traditionally closes the MotoGP season as the season finale venue.
overview: Circuit Ricardo Tormo is a 4.005 km permanent circuit near the city of Valencia in eastern Spain. Named after Ricardo Tormo, a Spanish motorcycle racer who died of leukaemia in 1998, the circuit hosts MotoGP's Valencia Grand Prix — the final round of the season for much of the 2000s and 2010s — as well as Formula E and WorldSBK. Hot, grippy, and technical, it is a circuit that has hosted numerous championship deciders.
history: Circuit Ricardo Tormo opened in 1999, purpose-built for MotoGP after Valencia's enthusiasm for the sport demanded a dedicated venue. It was immediately added to the MotoGP calendar. The decision to host the MotoGP season finale here for over a decade turned Valencia into one of the most emotionally charged rounds of the year — multiple world championships have been won and lost at this circuit. Formula E added a Valencia ePrix to its calendar. WorldSBK has also used Valencia regularly.
notable_moments: Valentino Rossi's devastating 2015 collision with Marc Marquez, costing him the championship to Jorge Lorenzo. Multiple MotoGP championship deciders held here when the Valencia round was the season finale. Jorge Lorenzo's emotional victories in front of his home Valencian crowd. The 2006 MotoGP finale where Rossi secured the title. Formula E ePrix events in the warm Valencian climate.

name: Circuit de Vila Real
bio: The Circuito Internacional de Vila Real has held road racing in the streets of the Portuguese city since 1931. At 4.785km it is one of the longer active street circuits in European motorsport, and has hosted the TCR World Tour since 2015.
overview: Circuit de Vila Real is a 4.793 km street circuit through the northern Portuguese city of Vila Real. One of the most dangerous-looking circuits used in top-level touring car racing, it runs through narrow stone-walled streets with no run-off, barriers centimetres from the racing line, and a tunnel section. The WTCR has used Vila Real as one of its most distinctive and challenging events. Drivers universally regard it as demanding maximum commitment — there is no forgiveness if you get it wrong.
history: Vila Real has hosted motorsport events on its city streets since the 1930s, making it one of the longest-running circuit locations in Portugal. The modern WTCR era brought the circuit back to prominence on the international touring car calendar. The circuit winds through the heart of the town, past historic granite buildings and over the narrow bridges of the city's hill. Its character is closer to the street circuits of the 1950s and 1960s than to any modern facility.
notable_moments: WTCR rounds producing dramatic incidents given the circuit's zero-tolerance barrier walls. The visual spectacle of touring cars threading through medieval Portuguese streetscapes. The circuit's longevity — over 90 years of motorsport use on essentially the same roads.

name: Virginia International Raceway
bio: Virginia International Raceway sits in the Piedmont countryside and has been a club racing landmark since 1957. Its multiple configurations and smooth surface made it a hidden gem before professional series rediscovered it.
overview: Virginia International Raceway is a 5.265 km permanent road course in the rural southern Virginia foothills near Alton — one of America's most scenically beautiful circuit environments. The circuit winds through the Virginia countryside with long, flowing sections and demanding elevation changes. IMSA's Michelin GT Challenge at VIR is a mid-season fixture, and the circuit is highly regarded by drivers and teams for its combination of technical challenge and natural setting.
history: VIR opened in 1957 and hosted major American road racing events in the 1960s before closing. It was revived in 2000 after restoration, returning as a modern permanent facility. The circuit's isolated location in rural Virginia — far from major population centres — gives race weekends a distinctive atmosphere, drawing a committed fan base willing to travel for what many consider one of the best road courses in the United States.
notable_moments: IMSA's Michelin GT Challenge at VIR producing competitive GT class battles. The circuit's revival from abandonment as a model for historic American road courses returning to use. Drivers' frequent citation of VIR as one of North America's most technically enjoyable circuits.

name: Watkins Glen International
bio: The Glen has been at the heart of American road racing since 1948, when the original race ran on public streets through the town. The permanent circuit cut through the New York hills since 1956 and hosted F1 from 1961 to 1980.
overview: Watkins Glen International is a 5.435 km permanent road circuit in the Finger Lakes wine region of upstate New York. One of America's great road courses, it produced some of F1's most dramatic early American Grands Prix (1961-1980) and remains a fixture for NASCAR and IMSA. The Bus Stop chicane, the long back straight, and the Esses provide a demanding, diverse lap. The natural setting in the hills above Seneca Lake gives the venue a quality unusual among American permanent circuits.
history: Watkins Glen hosted its first sports car race in 1948 using the public streets of the village — before the permanent circuit was built in 1956. The circuit hosted the United States Grand Prix in Formula 1 from 1961 to 1980, making it the longest-tenured US Grand Prix venue. F1 left Watkins Glen after 1980 as the circuit struggled financially. NASCAR arrived in 1986 and the annual Go Bowling at The Glen became one of the Cup schedule's two road course events. The circuit was rebuilt and expanded over the following decades.
notable_moments: The 1973 US Grand Prix where François Cevert died in qualifying and Tyrrell withdrew — one of F1's most tragic weekends. Peter Revson's 1973 US GP victory. Mario Andretti's victories at his home circuit in the F1 era. Jeff Gordon's multiple Watkins Glen NASCAR wins, establishing himself as the premier road course specialist of his generation.

name: Yas Marina Circuit
bio: Abu Dhabi's season finale venue opened in 2009 beneath the Yas Viceroy hotel. A 2022 layout revision transformed it from a processional into one of the genuinely more exciting circuits on the calendar.
overview: Yas Marina Circuit is a 5.281 km permanent circuit on Yas Island, Abu Dhabi, built around a marina and dominated by the Yas Viceroy Hotel — a building straddling the circuit on which the hotel's façade lights up in reaction to race results. The host of F1's Abu Dhabi Grand Prix since 2009, it has traditionally been the season finale, making it the venue where Formula 1 world champions have most often been crowned or denied. A significant circuit revision in 2021 opened up overtaking opportunities that the original layout lacked.
history: Yas Marina Circuit was designed by Hermann Tilke and opened in 2009 as the centrepiece of Abu Dhabi's Yas Island entertainment and tourism development. The circuit immediately hosted the F1 season finale — a position it has held almost continuously. The 2021 revision shortened and opened up the Sector 3 section, replacing a tight chicane sequence with faster corners that allow closer racing. The construction of the circuit alongside a hotel, theme park, and marina reflects the Abu Dhabi government's strategy of using motorsport as anchor infrastructure for a broader leisure development.
notable_moments: The 2010 season finale where Fernando Alonso finished seventh — enough to win the championship if results had gone differently, but Sebastian Vettel took the title. The 2016 finale where Nico Rosberg secured his only championship against Lewis Hamilton. The 2021 Abu Dhabi Grand Prix — the most controversial race in modern F1 history: a late safety car, a disputed restart procedure that allowed Max Verstappen to overtake Lewis Hamilton on the final lap, and a title outcome that divided the sport. Rosberg's shock retirement announcement the week after his 2016 victory here.

name: Circuit Zandvoort
bio: The Dutch Grand Prix returned in 2021 after a 36-year absence. Zandvoort's banked Hugenholtz and Arie Luyendyk corners are unique in modern F1, and 100,000 orange-clad fans make it the loudest race weekend in the sport.
overview: Circuit Zandvoort is a 4.259 km circuit in the dunes behind the North Sea coast resort of Zandvoort. First built in 1948, it is one of the most atmospheric circuits in European motorsport — narrow, fast, and enclosed by sand dunes that create a unique claustrophobic intensity. The rebuilt circuit that returned to F1 in 2021 added two banked corners, including a 19-degree banking at the final turn, that allow cars to carry extraordinary speed into the pit straight. The Orange Army — Max Verstappen's fanbase — transform race weekends here into something unlike any other event on the calendar.
history: Zandvoort hosted its first Dutch Grand Prix in 1952 and became a regular F1 fixture through the late 1970s and 1980s. It was dropped from the F1 calendar after 1985. The circuit continued hosting national motorsport through the intervening decades. The revival was driven by Max Verstappen's emergence as a title contender — demand from Dutch fans for an F1 home race forced the schedule discussion. The circuit was extensively rebuilt: a banking added to the Hugenholtz corner, the circuit widened in critical sections, and a new banked final corner constructed. The Dutch GP returned in 2021.
notable_moments: Niki Lauda winning here in 1985 for McLaren. Gilles Villeneuve's astonishing wet qualifying lap in 1979. Max Verstappen's dominant home victories since 2021. The extraordinary atmosphere created by the Orange Army — coordinated by colour, song, and noise, creating a wall of sound unlike any other F1 race weekend. The dune setting, unique in European motorsport.

name: Zhuzhou International Circuit
bio: Zhuzhou International Circuit in Hunan province is a permanent motorsport facility in central China, serving the country's expanding domestic racing market.
overview: Zhuzhou International Circuit is a permanent circuit in Zhuzhou, an industrial city in Hunan Province, central China. Used for WTCR (World Touring Car Cup) rounds as part of the championship's engagement with China's growing motorsport market, Zhuzhou represents the expansion of international touring car competition into China's inland provinces beyond the coastal cities that typically host major international events.
history: Zhuzhou International Circuit was developed to bring international motorsport to the Xiangjiang River valley region of Hunan, a province known primarily for its role as the birthplace of Mao Zedong and its spicy cuisine rather than motorsport. The WTCR's Chinese rounds — rotating between multiple circuits — reflected the championship's commercial partnership with China's automotive industry and the ambition to develop a domestic motorsport fanbase across the country's interior.
notable_moments: WTCR rounds at Zhuzhou contributing to the championship's Chinese market engagement. International touring car competition in the Hunan interior, bringing world-class motorsport to a region without prior major circuit racing heritage.

name: Biķernieki Circuit
bio: The Biķernieki Complex in Riga is Latvia's premier motorsport venue, hosting rallycross, circuit racing, and karting events within a large forested park.
overview: Biķernieki Circuit is a motorsport complex in the Biķernieki forest on the eastern edge of Riga, Latvia's capital. It is one of the oldest motorsport venues in the Baltic states, combining a permanent road circuit with facilities for rallycross, karting, and other disciplines. The rallycross track has been used for World Rallycross Championship rounds, making Biķernieki one of the few Baltic venues to host a world championship event.
history: Motorsport in the Biķernieki forest dates back to the Soviet era, when the circuit was used for national and pan-Soviet competitions. The venue has been continuously developed since Latvian independence in 1991. The rallycross facility brought international attention when it hosted World RX rounds, placing Latvia on the global motorsport calendar. The natural forest setting gives the complex a distinctive character — the trees line the track closely, creating a contained, atmospheric environment unique among European venues.
notable_moments: World Rallycross Championship rounds at the circuit bringing world-class competitors to Riga. The circuit's historical role as the premier motorsport venue for the Baltic states throughout the Soviet era and into independence.

name: Nyirád Racing Centre
bio: The Nyirád Racing Centre is a compact, purpose-built rallycross facility in western Hungary, known for its mix of asphalt and gravel surfaces.
overview: Nyirád Racing Centre is a motorsport facility in the Bakony hills of western Hungary, near the town of Nyirád in Veszprém County. A smaller venue within the Hungarian motorsport network, it supplements the Hungaroring and Balaton Park as one of the country's permanent circuit facilities, hosting national championships and local events in the hill country of Transdanubia.
history: Nyirád Racing Centre is part of Hungary's broader investment in regional motorsport infrastructure. The Bakony region's elevated, forested landscape provides an appealing environment for a circuit venue distinct from the flat plains where the Hungaroring is located. The facility hosts Hungarian national championships and forms part of the country's motorsport development pathway.
notable_moments: Hungarian national championship rounds providing racing development opportunities in the Bakony region.

name: Höljes Motorstadion
bio: Höljes Motorstadion in rural Värmland, Sweden, is widely regarded as the spiritual home of rallycross, drawing massive crowds for the annual Magic Weekend.
overview: Höljes Motorstadion is a 1.013 km rallycross circuit deep in the forests of Värmland, Sweden — a tiny village in the wilderness three hours north of Gothenburg. It is the most atmospheric venue on the World Rallycross Championship calendar, regularly described by drivers as their favourite event of the year. The Höljes RallyX consistently draws crowds of over 40,000 to a village of fewer than 200 inhabitants, with fans camping in the forest for the entire weekend.
history: Höljes has hosted rallycross events since 1967, making it one of the oldest dedicated rallycross venues in the world. The circuit has hosted the Swedish round of the European and World Rallycross Championship continuously, maintaining its position even as the championship has modernised and the car classes have changed from traditional internal combustion to electric. The local Larsson family, who run the event, have maintained the festival character while upgrading facilities for world championship standards.
notable_moments: Timmy and Kevin Hansen's victories at their home event, racing in front of tens of thousands of fans in the Värmland forest. Petter Solberg's multiple World RX wins at Höljes. The electric World RX era's first Höljes round, where the sound of the forest changed as electric rallycross cars replaced combustion engines. The festival camping culture that makes Höljes unique in European motorsport.

name: Mondello Park
bio: Mondello Park is Ireland's only permanent motor racing circuit, located in County Kildare, and hosts a wide variety of national and international events.
overview: Mondello Park is a 3.441 km permanent circuit near Naas in County Kildare, Ireland — Ireland's only permanent motorsport facility at international grade. It hosts Irish national championships, international Superbike events, and occasional rounds of British national championships. The circuit sits in the flat Kildare countryside, 40km southwest of Dublin, and is Ireland's motorsport heartbeat.
history: Mondello Park opened in 1968, built on the Mondello estate by Martin Birrane — who later also owned Le Mans circuit in France and saved it from financial difficulty in the 1980s. The circuit has been continuously used since for Irish national championships and international events. The circuit has hosted occasional British touring car and single-seater events when calendar geography supports it.
notable_moments: Decades of Irish national motorsport history. Martin Birrane's ownership connecting Mondello to Le Mans in an unusual cross-European motorsport link. The circuit's role as the sole development ground for Irish motorsport talent aspiring to European championships.

name: Circuit de Lohéac
bio: Circuit de Lohéac in Brittany is France's most celebrated rallycross venue, renowned for its passionate spectators and demanding mixed-surface layout.
overview: Circuit de Lohéac is a 1.235 km mixed-surface rallycross circuit in the commune of Lohéac in Brittany, France. It is one of the most celebrated venues in European rallycross — the Bretagne World RX of France traditionally draws enormous crowds from across western France, and Lohéac's rural Breton setting has made it a festival event as much as a race meeting. The combined tarmac and gravel surface is a genuine driving test demanding both smoothness and aggression.
history: Lohéac has hosted rallycross events since the 1980s and has been a fixture on the World Rallycross Championship calendar. The event grew from a local Breton motorsport tradition into one of the anchor rounds of the world championship. The circuit's intimate scale — 1.235 km around an agricultural landscape — contrasts with the spectacle of the World RX cars it hosts. The Lohéac weekend is known for its festival atmosphere, camping, and the passionate French rallycross fanbase that treats it as an annual pilgrimage.
notable_moments: World RX championship rounds that have served as title deciders. Sébastien Loeb's appearances in World RX drawing mainstream media attention to the championship. The Lohéac event's reputation as the most atmospherically distinctive round on the World RX calendar.

name: Circuito de Lousada
bio: The Circuito de Lousada is a compact rallycross and circuit venue in northern Portugal, long associated with the country's strong rallying and rallycross culture.
overview: Circuito de Lousada is a 1.267 km rallycross circuit near Porto in northern Portugal. One of the traditional European rallycross venues, Lousada combines tarmac and gravel in a compact layout that rewards precise car control and aggressive driving. Its position in the productive agricultural Tâmega Valley and its proximity to Porto have made it a well-supported event on the World Rallycross calendar.
history: Lousada has been a rallycross circuit since the 1980s and has hosted World RX rounds for many years. The circuit's compact nature means racing is invariably close — there is no room to build large gaps, and every mistake is immediately punished by a competitor going past. Portugal's passion for rallying and rallycross, inherited from decades of WRC presence in the country, gives Lousada a devoted and enthusiastic spectator base.
notable_moments: World Rallycross Championship rounds at Lousada contributing to title battles. The circuit's role as a Portuguese motorsport institution alongside the rally special stages that run through the same region.

name: Jakarta International E-Prix Circuit
bio: The Jakarta International E-Prix Circuit is a temporary street circuit in the Indonesian capital, built across the Ancol waterfront area. Originally constructed for Formula E, it hosts the 2026 FIA Rallycross World Cup.
overview: The Jakarta International E-Prix Circuit is a 2.369 km street circuit on the waterfront of Ancol in North Jakarta, Indonesia. Used for the Formula E Jakarta ePrix since 2022, it was the first world championship motorsport event held in Indonesia since the Indonesian Motorcycle Grand Prix era. The circuit runs along the Jakarta Bay waterfront, with the Java Sea as a backdrop and the sprawling Indonesian capital surrounding it. Jakarta's 30+ million metropolitan population makes the ePrix one of Formula E's highest-profile urban events.
history: Indonesia's return to the world motorsport calendar came via Formula E's strategy of bringing electric racing to major Asian cities. The Jakarta ePrix was announced as part of Indonesia's government-backed effort to attract international sporting events, coinciding with the country's hosting ambitions around the 2023 ASEAN Games and other major events. The circuit uses the Ancol Dreamland entertainment and waterfront complex, repurposed for race weekend. Extensive marshalling and crowd management logistics are required for an event in one of the world's most densely populated cities.
notable_moments: Formula E's 2022 Jakarta ePrix — Indonesia's return to world motorsport after decades. The visual spectacle of electric racing cars on the Jakarta waterfront with the Java Sea behind them. The circuit's role in Formula E's strategy of anchoring major championship rounds in Southeast Asian megacities.

name: Adelaide Street Circuit
bio: Victoria Park in North Adelaide hosts the Supercars season finale, the bp Adelaide Grand Final. The compact street layout runs the perimeter roads of the park with temporary barriers, bringing championship-deciding racing to the heart of the South Australian capital.
overview: The Adelaide Street Circuit runs through the Park Lands that ring Adelaide's CBD — a 3.219 km temporary circuit constructed on the public roads and park roads of the city's inner green belt. It hosted Formula 1's Australian Grand Prix from 1985 to 1995, becoming one of the most beloved circuits on that era's calendar before F1 moved to Melbourne. Today it is the host of the Supercars Adelaide 500, one of the marquee events of the Australian touring car season. The palm-lined streets, the compressed barriers, and the city-centre atmosphere make Adelaide one of the standout events on any Supercars calendar.
history: Adelaide first hosted the Australian F1 Grand Prix in 1985, the final round of the season — a role it played until 1995 when Victoria's government successfully bid for the race and moved it to Albert Park. The F1 years produced some of the most dramatic season finales in the sport's history. After F1's departure, the circuit continued hosting Supercars events. The Adelaide 500 was cancelled from 2012 to 2020 before a successful revival brought the event back to the calendar with renewed enthusiasm.
notable_moments: The 1986 Australian GP finale — Nigel Mansell's tyre blowout at 300 km/h that handed the championship to Alain Prost. The 1994 Australian GP, Schumacher and Hill's collision that decided the most controversial championship of the modern era. The 2010 Supercars Adelaide 500 finale. The circuit's revival as a Supercars event after its years away — testament to Adelaide's identity as a motorsport city.
